-The approach of Christmas thas had 
~a very appreciable effect in banishing 
strife from the Legislative chamber. 
‘Whether the Christmas spiit has real- 
-ly been getting in its work or the 
~members simply want to be free to get 
“back to their private occupations after 
the. holidays, is open to conjecture. 


But the fact is that there has been, 


elittle unnecessary: sauabbling during 
the past week and a great deal of 
teally hard work. 


The government has.come through 
the session with a. fairly cafe ma- 
-jority at its back and “there is little 
‘prospect Of an early appeal to the 
-Neople being made. - ‘It is -centain inat 


aniuty of the-members who: supseried . 


ats policy did -7 simply: because. ti: 
‘were callei. woe to choose betw « 
what appeared to them as.two evils. 
They could; not help ‘recognizing the 
«dangers into which ¢he premier was 
leading. them: and. the province and 
yet they dreaded: what: would happen 
-in case everything were -thrown into 
the melting-pot of. a general election. 


The Saturday News is ofthe opinion 


that such temporizing cannot do them 
and their party any good and. that 


_ when the results of Mr. Sifton’s alto- 


:gether unprecedented and unjustifiable 

Jegislation begin to-be felt. the’ last 
“state of his followers. wil! be very 
‘much worse tion the first. 


It. has. already’ been. shown how far 
‘the passing..of<premicr’s bill diverting 
the &. and G. W. funds is from being 
an. end-of the-whole-matter. This 
week two other bills were introduced 
“by the leader of the House which were 
‘in themselves complete admission’ of 
‘the correctness of two of the conten- 
‘tions.of those who have offered oppo- 
sition to him. 
> One provided for tlc compensation 
of those who had claims against the 
‘company. But if the legislature in 
-dealing with the company did what 
it had a perfect right to do, if, as 
the premier alleged, it was simply fore 
closing a mortgage, what excuse is 
there for taking funds of the province 
to reimburse those who had had no 
dealings with the province? > 

The explanation is, of course, not 
‘a difficult one. The premier recoz- 
~mises, as everybody else does, that his 
declaration about a mortgage. being 
foreclosed, was simply a pleasant lic- 
tle fiction to cover up a very discredit- 
sable act, an act which is unique in 
‘Canadian political annals and whch 
is properly designated as confiscation 
.and nothing else. However, the funds 


%n2332939333229333799303900393 CEFEEECEECERECEECEECER? * 


ing the amount of the A. and G.. W. 
bond. sale proceeds on the security 
of the general revenue of the province. 
The disposal of this sum provided for 
in tthe estimates, but, it is apparent, 
the government is by no means sure. 
that it will be able to ge. ‘old of the 
money from. :e banks, aid <> las 
felt it necessary tq introduce this sup- 
plementary bill... The province will 
accordingly be liable, not only on the 
A. and G. W. bonds, the’ diversion of 
the proceeds of which Mr, Clarke 
stands a very good chance of prevent-

THE HEROISM OF THE COM- 
MON MAN. 


The terrific mining disaster at the 
village of Bellevue in the Crow's Nest, 
in which some twenty-five young men 
lost their lives, is the first of its pro 
portions in the annals of the indus- 
-ty in this province and it is sencerely 
to be hoped that it will be a long time 
before there is another like it. Mine 
owners complain from time to time 
regarding the restrictions that are 
placed pon ‘them but it is evident that

say that its efforts have not been ap-

preciated by the citizens.

test and the tthree others who offered 
themselves for re-clection were re- 
turned. Alderman Lundy made a 
strong play with his park policy ant 
vow that he has had the satisfaction 
of being placed at the head of the poll, 
he should push ahead his ideas for all 
that they are worth. Alderman Lr

Washington, bit for a similar amount 
which it is raising by ths latest mea- 
sure. A person does not need to be 
a financier to realize what a. glorious 
mix-up all this is likely to lead to. 
The discussion on the budget show- 
ed that, however great the troubles 
from without the premier has laid up 
for himself by his legislation, they are 
by no means his only ones. Two of 
the members, Messrs. Shaw and Stauf. 
fer, who supported the original bill, 
voted against the ministry when it 
came to actnally disposing of the 
funds that had been intended for the 


tails of the government’s expenditure 
Mr. Bennett and Mr. Michener, by the 
attention which ithey gave to the var- 


ious’ items in-the estimates, when the 


House went into committee on supply 
showed that Alberta can henceforth 
count on having very efficient watch- 
dogs of the treasury. The absence 9{ 
this at previous sessions was a con- 
stant source of danger. 

All week long the public has been 
looking for something to transpire in 
connection with the charges made in 
the newspapers against Mr. Cushing.

that’ if it, had been paid proper heed

be made of those responsible. A few 
years ago, a commission, of which the 
present provincial premier was chalr-

of mining, conditions in the province

tho citizens in dts arth ce tewards Bae 
trenton, will hen elorth be found pres 
rered to negotiate with a view vo. 
wi tds accomplishing the most impors 
tan object of po: y Before etther ity 
that of tthe amalgamation of the twin 
municipalities. There is everything 
to gain for both, heir interests aro 
identical and within the next year they 
will become in all other respects one 
community, ‘The folly of keeping 
up the organizations and of fostering 
expensive rivalrie? which have no 
reasonable basis is rapidly becoming 
apparent to everyone, ‘Nae: fortunes 
must ultimately be jolmed. = Why 
should they not get together with the 
least possible delay so that they may 
secure at an early date the advantages 
to be derived from ths move? Partie 
eularly desirable is it thas the union 
should be consummitel before the 
taking of tthe Dominion census nert 
June, ‘Mhere is the giemtest uced that 
the best possible saewlag should then 
be made. Once the two cities agree an 
order, in cotuneil can give effect to 
their wishes,  ‘I‘his was made clear 
the other day in the Legisinture, Why 
should not negotiations be opened 
immediately?

WINNIPEG’S BUSINESS SECTION 


2 ANA bS mnAerY i 


‘The story of young Alderson, who 
n the effort to rescue soine of the im- 
ptisoned miners, sacrificed himself, 
giving up his helmét to an unknowa 
Italian and then being overcome by 
the poisonots fumes, stirs the blood 
and gives us a ttotally new conception 
of the innante nobility of humanity. 
How can a person be a misanthrope 
wth such an incident before him. {J 
have ever hated all nations, profes- 


die, De 


when one’s experience is limited to 
men living under artificial condtions, 
But when they are brought to ithe test 
which a time of stress imposes, it is 
astonishing whait a large proportion 
of them ring absolutely true, ‘The 
dull, uninteresting fellow we knock 
up against every day may be the most 
genuine hero, only lacking the oppor- 
tunity to have great, almost god-like 
qualities, brought to the proof. 


(Continued on page eight 


piulation With a loss of $35 per day 
on the packing plant line and $17 on 
the Iirst Street, itis absolutely neces- 
sary to go slow with ofer projects,

reserve makes the problerd of street 
railway adiministration difficu't, as it 
does most other civic proolems, Tf 
that great unoceuped area in the city! 
heant were filled up, a belt ne could 
be established witea weoull helo she 
departiment’s rev+yi'3 very materially,

of what 


To Covent Garden people stream 
To drink the music there; 


We stand along the curb and dream 


To melodies more rare; 
Sing on! enchanted minstrel-girl, 
Thou artless, young and fair! 


The ‘buses of Southampton Row, 
The jingling hansoms here, 
Bear London, heedless, to and fr9 
In searon of evening cheer; 
* For us, thou art enough, dear voice 
Forgetful—sweet and clear! 


Our day-long toil but goes to win 
Another toilsome day; 

Play on, oblivious violin! 
Soit harp, beseech thee, play! 

And thou, pale girl with eyes aflanie. 
Sing on for us who stay! 


—Arthur Upson, in The Bellman. 


The other evening I happened -o 
be sitting by one of the Judges in 
the Journal's Christmas Story Com- 
petiton, as he sat engrossed in his 
work, 

Speaking to him once or twice and 
receiving no answer, I idly picked 
up one of the manuscripts to sze 
wherein lay the secret of his absorp- 
tion. 

Last year, quite candidly, I didn‘: 
take much interest in the contribu- 
tions. There was about them an im- 
maturity, if I may speak frankly. an 
unfinished, careless, frothy character 
that argued ill for any promise of a 


permanent contribution to Westera 
Canadian Iiterature. 
They were disappointing — Unc 


gets to look for big,heartsome thing: 
in this land of vast distances and tre- 
mendous potentialities. 

There are so many subjects thar 
lend themselves to story-writing in 
this particular part of the country 
that someone seemed almost bound to 
strike a happy note. 

Prospecting, homesteading, the ex- 
perienoss of the inexperienced in the 
construction camps or in the little 
new-made towns along the railroads. 
Stories of booms and startling real- 
estate transactions. The romances 
in the lives of some of the human 
driftwood that is distributed in farms 
and towns of this greedy insatiable 
West land. The tragedy tha: stalks 
always where building is being done, 
and new trails ure being blazed. 

Weil last year somehow everyone 
seemed to muff it. 


In the eyes of the conglomerous 
flotsam and jetsam that throng our 
highways and by-ways, these youny 
writers found no real inspiration; is 
the strange tongues they heard on 
every side, they caught no echo of 
the hope and faith that drove these 
men and women of foreign birth to 
a land of which they actually knew 
nothing, save only by report. 

At every door and window, Oppor- 
tunity knocked, but they barred the 
entrances, and were content to burn 
the midnight oil. 

The literature of the West will 
never be written after this fashion. 
It must be drenched with the free air 
of the prairies, it must strike deep 
down into the heart of things. It 
must deal with che big broad essen- 
itals of living, it must tell of hope de- 
ferred that maketh the heart sick, 
and men that are men, be they good 
or bad, and women with che makings 
of martyrs 

The girl with che faraway look in 
her eyes and the lace shawl draped 
coquettishly over her shoulders has 
no place here. Pink tea drivel. anc 
smirt society utterances in such a 
setting will be meaningless But the 
men and women facing the Why and 
How of existene:, will rub shoulders 
with he man who digs in ¢he ditch 
for a liv. « ++i he and she 
come out here to cover graves 
their hearts and lives. 

The first story I picked up curisus- 
ly enough turned on of these 
themes. It was an Indian story. the 
tale ef a young squaw who had wu 
drink the bitter of 


ha) 


one 


ot 


deen waters 


life. But che joy I sook in reading 
that simpre, beavtifully-told recorc 
ef a woman's love for husband and 


child—why it was one of the best 
Moments of my life in the West. The 
next onc—for I was lured on and on 
—was equally good literature. 
Mirrorites this news t) 
you. We have in this little crop of 
Stories the best harvest this magni- 
ficent land has yet yielded. For ‘t 
shows us that while inen and women 
have digged and planted sh 


good 


enil, 


MINSTRELS IN BLOOMSBERRY 


who haa} 


| 

they have not let their hearts = 
brains lie fallow. 
But the smack of the West thers 
is in the yarns is what I like most. 
Not Gilbert Parker's drawing-room: 


used to from Fly-by- 
story-writers. 


have

stretched 


grown 
artists, and 
unutterable woe hears. 
to breaking peint in tke 
silences: the deep and abiding joy 
of men who once were lost and have 


of 


second-hand knowledge. Not all th 
mis-siatements and exaggerations we 
found themselves. | 

Oh, I look for wonderful things in 
the future, from these men and wo- 
men who sing of the plough. the 
threshing-outfit. the camp, and «he 
natural free life. of a young and 
growing land. facts can do.

in too may cases as the genuine ar-) Deby, my cld ‘friend down. ure line, 
ticle, and it means that the credit will that he has a_ real treat in store «his 


Of course I knew it all before. But 
concrete incidents have ever a way 
of pointing a moral as uo abstract

be largely due where it so often’ be- | Christmas, and tu wish him incident-| from the neal thing as the tale of the 


lengs — to a modest unpretentious |ally the compliments of the season. 
newspaper. backcd by a live editor 
who beliewss he has a duty to the ‘these days in the magazincs. don't

SHAVING and SMOKING OUTFITS

THE REAL HOLD-UP OF A 
STAGE COACH. 


Fact as Contrasted With Fiction. 


Of all the tales of Western lie 
there’s none that differs so widely 


stage-coach hold-up,. says an’ old 


They do make Santa “live-looking frontiersman. He is quoted in the 


New York Times as follows: 


“The breathless reader sees vividly 
the outlaws lying in wait along the 
edge of the ‘iiesa’-—bluff’ is too com- 
mon a word to use—while the coach 
toils up from the © ‘arroyo’—so.. is 
‘gulch’—the leader, well masked in 
his ‘poncho’ .. likewise is ‘cloak’— 
carefully surveying the doomed ve- 
hicle. Then comes~ the attack, the 
killing of the driver, the wild pur- 
suit, the slaying of tthe leader of the 
hand by the brave young mining en- 
gineer, who thas thrown’ himself: in. 
front of the beautiful millionaire’s 
daughter just in time to save’ her life 
(and get a bullet through his arm) 
that she nurses through\to marriage, 
the repulse of the outlaws, etc., ali 
Plentifully garnished yith mongrel 
Mexican terms that are never heard 
except on the melodrama — stage .or 
seen outside the tale aforesaid, 


“Now in real life there is seldom 
any shooting in a stage-coach rob- 
bery. One thing is certain, the dri- 
ver never gets shot. The driver is 
never even shot at, for he very sensi- 
bly puts up his hands, knowing ho4 
suicidal would be any attempt at re- 
sistance against armed outiaws, and 
he sitting up there as pretty a ‘ar- 
get as one ever saw in a shooting 
gallery, for that would mean a run- 
away team—of all things what the 
robbers don’t want; they’ve got en- 
ough to keep them busy, as it 1s. 
Perhaps, I cannot illustrate better 
'what occurs at a real stage robbery 
than by giving you my expcrience 
when I was driving a stage through 
Southern Idaho in the early ‘'80’s. 


“I was driving from Shoshone to 
Boise City, and about dusk one even- 
ing was going down the canon roa‘} 
that leads from Camas prairie to the 
headwaters of the Payette — not a 
settler for miles around. There was 
only one woman from New England, 
who was going out to Seattle .o 
teach school. She had never been 
West of the Hudson River before. 

“Now, while the country she had 
come from and that she was going 
to wene civilised all right, the strip 
in Southern Idaho we were then ¢ra- 
velling over was pretty wild. But J 
didn’t say a word to her about it for 
fear of frightening the little thing. 

“She was sitting up on the box 
with me and aumiring the sunset 
tints in the Western sky, when as we 
swung arotind a bend in the canon, a 
man standing in the middle of the 
road about fifty feet ahead, with a 
Winchester rifle lying in the hollow 
of his arm, flung up his hand to me. 
le was the first human being we had 
teen since we left Shoshone at day- 
break ¢hat morning though that wasn't 
the reason I threw my leaders back 
on their haunches, flung ‘on the 
brake, and came to a standsiill. 

“'Throw out the mail sackY order- 
cd the man with the rifle. 

“IT reached down and flung the 
sack into the road. 

““Better throw out the Wells-Far- 
go box, too, I guess,” he added. 

“J kicked over the treasure box as 
ordered. 

“*That’s all,” said the man curtly, 
‘Drive on.’ 

“I touched up the team and we 
went bowling along merrily down the 
canon. There was not a word spok- 
in for a full ten minutes. Then the 
young woman, turning to me, said 
zuilessly: 

““‘That looked a mighty lonesome 
place to have a post-offiee.’ ”

THE SATURDAY Bes 


Home and Society - 


The unexpected has happened, and. 


this week, far from being devoted to 
Christmas preparations, ‘has | turned 
out an exceptionally busy one socially. 

On:Monday Mrs. Dickey had a very 
enjoyable tea. in her ,apartmenis at 
the Arlington. Wednesday Mrs. 
Cautley of Belton Lodge, had an after- 
noon. bridge, and Mrs. Howard Rit- 
chie an At Home. On,Thursday Mrs. 
Muir Frith had a charming tea in hon- 
or of Mrs. Hal Burnham, one of the 
recent brides. The same night so- 
ciety iturned owt, en masse, to th: 
amateur production of “The Liars.” 
Friday, Mrs. James. Biggar has a 
house-warming tea in her :delightful 
new home, and “The Liars” again 
plays to a crowded house in the even- 
in. Saturday Mrs. Frank Smith has a 
smart matinee bridge at the King Ed- 
ward Hotel, and at night'there will be 
a crowded assemblage of prominent 
society folk and music at the Kocian- 
Eisner recital. 


Altogether it has meant a big scram- 
ble to keep one’s engagements, as in 
addition, Mrs, Norman Soars held her 
first reception since her marriage on 


Mrs. Garnet Morris and Mrs. Hugth 
Campbell presided at the  tea-table, 
and, Mrs. Dickins served ‘the ices, 
while Miss Viva Sommerville, Miss 
McKenny, Miss Kate Lowes, and 
Miss Bessie Scott were four attentive 
assistants. : 

The tea-table was a lovely arrange- 
ment of pink carnations and fern, and 
shaded candle-lights. 


—— ee 


If you are out Christmassing, as a 
matter of course you drop into Jim 
Mah Wath’s, the little Chinese shop of 
quaint brasses and Chinese toys, and 
silks and “culios.” 


If you know Jim, the “vella smart” 


Satsuma novelties, dainty china, ivor- 
ies, and queer little secret boxes and 
things, that fairly make the pennies 
walk out of your purse. 

This year too, walks sturdily about 
a little “Jim,” answering to the name 
of “Pan,” loking sweet enough to be 
Peter Pan! what at. four years of 
age, has come out to Canada with 
his mother, to grow up ‘among the 


1 a Se ee —_—_—_—_——— 


“ALICE KENNEDY AND EDWAR D VAIL 


In Carmen to be presented at the Em pire Theatre. 


Thursday, and being so popular a 
young bride, everyone was cager to 
call. The same day too, the first Curl- 
ing Tea of the season drew its little 
circle of devotees. It is always the 
same in Edmonton, it never rains but 
what it pours, but somehow, some- 
way people seem tto take it all in, and 
be none the worse for the downp ut 


Madame Thibandeau rteurned on 
Thursday last, after a two months’ 
sojourn at the coast, looking radiant- 
ly well for her prolonged visit to that 
most lovely spot. 


Mr. and Mrs. F. C. Lowes and Mr. 
W. L. Walsh, K.C., came up from Cal- 
gary on Monday morning, and were 
gitests at the King Edward, that very 
popular hostelry for Legislative visi- 
tots and the cream of the travelling 
public. 


Mons. and Madame Martin are re- 
joicing in the birth of a wee daughter, 
who arrived late last week, 


Mrs. J. H. Morris was the hostess 
of a very jolly tea on Friday last, in 
honor of Mrs. Hill of Hillcrest, B.C., 
whose husband is one of the biggest 
coal magnates in the Province. 

The cheery, big living-room was 
gayly abloom with many lovely flow- 


ters for the occasion, and looked very 


attractive after a walk through the 
brisk early-evening air. 

Mrs. Morris received in a fascinat- 
ing pale pink chiffon frock, with love- 
ly lace and Paisley pattern trimmings. 
With her was ther mother, Mrs. Jar- 
dine, very sweet and dignified in silver 
grey silk with lace cap and acces3- 
sories, and Mrs. Hill had on an ultra 
smart. blak tailored suit, with bodice 
of black chiffon over green Paisley 
silk, her large black hat having a 
touch of the same green, with ermine 
introduced, the smart toute ensemble 
being further enhanced by some mag 
nificent Russian ermine furs and some 
fine jewels. 


Eenglish people, 
mers, 

If the shop doesn’t entice you, Pan 
will, and if you step inside it’s all over 
with you. Yau can’t nesist the ap- 
pealing Christmas gifts that surround 
you on every hand. 
everyone, from the biggest to the 
weeniest, It may be a beautiful Sat- 
suma hat-pin; maybe a quaint carved 
elephant. 


his father’s custo- 


The children are infatuat 


away across the seas from far-away 
China. As gifts to send back home 
you could find nothing that would 
be more appreciated. 

Jim is going to have a great big 
sale of silks, ete, from now until 
Christmas, with discounts varying 
from ten to forty per cent. [it will 
mean a unique chance to possess one- 
self of these attractive Oriental wanes, 
and doubtless Christmas eve will find 
Pan wondering ‘exceedingly, at the 
big bunch of Canadian money that has 
flowed into the little store’s coffers. 


(Continued on page 4.) 


FRED TERRY’S STEEL FLURRY. 


—e 


Fred Terry, the English actor, had 
luncheon the other day in a company, 
one of whom was a well known brok- 
er, and was moved to tell the story of 
an experience in stocks, only the call- 
ed them “shares,” after the English 
fashion, The broker repeated the 
story yesterday. 

Mr. Terry was interested when he 
learned the other was a broker, “Ah,” 
he said, “you have Sarge dealings in 
steels, T take it. 
that have the pictures of the miners 
and the pickaxes on the certificates, 
what? Ah, I thought so. Yes, steels. 
steels, that was it. I onee took the 
most curious sort of flier in steels. 

“One day several years ago an Eng- 
lialt friend, a chap who did a bit of 
speculating now and then and who 
kept in touch with the market, came to 
me in London and said: 

“Fred, have you any money?” 

“‘Not much, dear old chap,’ said J, 


z I'm rather hard up now.’ 


forgotten about those shares, until one OUR BEST SELLERS IN NEW FICTION SO

cays “culios” that he means enticing

“But what do T want with the bally 
things?” I cried. 

“Buy them and put them away and 
watch yourself get rich,’ he returned 

“Well, T wasn't very keen on the 
thing and I turned the matter over in 
my mind for some time. But T had 
reason have confidence in) my 
friend's judgment and none in my own 
in such a matter, so | bought that day. 
A day or two later I decided to look 
at the stock quotations. 


no

noyance steels had gone down a poins 
or two. Fact is they kept on going 
down until they reached 21, and vy 
that itime I was thoroughly disgust- 
ed with my venture and decided to 
put the share certificates away and 
forget them. T intended to lock them 
up in my strong box and thought | 
had done se.

The Famous Ra 


Is the Lamp ‘of Real Beauty 


because it gives the best light of all 
lamps. The Rayo gives a white, soft, 
mellow, diffused light—easy on the 
eye because it eann flicker. You 
can use your eyes as long as you wish 
‘under the Rayo light without strain. 

The Rayo Lamp. is low-priced, and 
even though you pay $5, $10 or $20 for 
other lamps, you may get more expensive 
decorations but you cannot get a better 
light than the low-priced Rayo gives. A 
strong, durable shade-holder holds the 
shade on firm and true. This season’s 
new burner adds strength and appearance. 


Once a Rayo User, Always One.

one day and told me to put them in 
there.’ 

“Get it out quick!’ I said, fle did 
so, and upon my word there in it, 
sute enough, were the certificates. 1 
took them and went ito a stockbroker’s 
office the next morning and sold them 
at 94, which was top price over there, 
I had gained £800 ($4,000) on my in- 
vestment. When [ next looked at 
the quotations steels had gone off. 

Sut when [ meet a stock broker I al- 
ways think of the pictures of the min-

A schoolboy, who had been vaccin- 


ated on the right arm, was given a 
tag, bearing the words, “I’ve been vac- 
cinated,” to ‘tie to his coat sleeve to 
mu ond Ins choolmates not to 
the tender arm, But ‘the lad proceed- 
ed to tie the tag on his left arm. 
“Why,” said the doctor, “you are put- 
ting it on the wrong arm!” “No,” 
said the boy: “you don't know the 


boys at our school!”

I regret that I have mislaid a letter 
received from a friend of the Mirror’s, 
asking that I publish a poem ersitle4 
“Lasca,” and—here again my memory 
fails me— one by Will Carleton. 

I have a fairly good library myself, 
but I found no trace of “Lasca”— 
and Will Carleton’s verses are so fu- 
gitive, being mainly contributed to 
magazines and daily papers, that, if 1 
could remember the particular poem 
acsked for, I despair of being able tc 
lay my hands on ¥ 

If my correspondent will be so good 


Jasper Place 
Sacrifice 


Must sell my beautiful lots in Jaz 
per Place at nearly what they cost 3 
years ago. 

Please let me explain this to you. 
It is no real estate scheme. 

Address Box 20.

REMINISCENCES. OF GOLDWIN 
SMITH. 


A rare picture of the old and the 
New is presented: in: “Reminiscences 
of Goldwin ‘Smith.’: 

The earliest recollections of the 
author were of the watchman calling 
the hour of. the night in his native 
town. of Reading; of mail coaches 
travelling on the Bath road “at the 
marvellous rate of twelve miles an 
hour”; of the toling.of the curfew 
from. the tower of old St. Lawrence 
Church; of the Radical member. for 
the borough ‘with a curious little hat 
stuck on his powdered head”; of his 
nurse lighting the fire with the tinder 
box; of a social circle in which a re- 
tired admiral who had fought under 


t 


Nelson, and a veteran gencral of the. 


Peninsula and ‘Waterloo formed a 
pant. 

“From this state of things I have 
lived ( the bulk of the ‘Reminscences’ 
began after the author had. passed his 
7sth year) into an age of express 
trains, ocean. greyhounds, electricty, 
and. general excitement and _restless- 
ness.” Between tthe state of things 
Pictured in the beginning and that 
portrayed at the end there is only a 
Single life time. “Yet I feel as if J 
were writing of antiquity. Whiths 
is the train rushing and where will 
the terminus be”? . 

One of the witticsms of the crcles 
which witnessed with understanding 
the beginning of the great author’s life

finishing photos.‘

rere THE SATURDAY NEWS , 


time is at the expense of Hallam. Thre 
historian was so given to contradic- 
tion it was said he had once got out 
of bed at night to contradict the 
watchman’s. statement of tthe hour 
and the weather, and Sydney Smith 
declared that the chief use of the 
electre telegraph would be “to enabla 
Hallam to contradict a man in Bir- 
mingham.” , 

One of tthe sturdy oaks that re- 
fused to change with the changing 
times was the Provost of Eton. 
Against change of any kind this ven- 
crable antique so completely set his 
face that it is said he would not open 
letters carried to him. by rail until 
the end of the time in which they, 
might have reached him by stage.

The judgments passed. on, and the 
stories told of the eminent and mighty

of a past generation in the “Reminise-

ruffling of feathers, Hayward, tho 
essayist, is quoted as saying that Pitt's 
last words were not, “Oh, my 
country”; but, “I think | could ear 
one of Bellamy’s meat pies.” “Can- 
onized for his virtues when he died 
the Prince Consort.” we are told, 
“while he lived was unpopular on ac- 
count of his manner, panticularly with 
women; he had, in his final perfection, 
the condescending manner of German 
royalty.” The late Queen Victoria 
on her marriage day “was dumpy, but 
comely, with a fresh complexion, low 
forehead, receding chin and promit 
ent eyes.” Rut, “notwithstanding 
her dumpiness she asquired a queen- 
ly bearing.” In her later years, we 
are further told, “she was made th: 
ebject of extravagant adulation; she

KOCIAN, THE GREAT 
VIOLINIST. 


Jaroslav Kocian, the most attractive 
of the many gifted Bohemian violin. 
ists, will be accorded the honor of be- 
ing the first great violin soloist here 
this season. Kocian’s art is unique, 
iv realises all the emotional values of 
the compositions he plays, as if he 
would invite his hearers to listen with 
him to the music, not to admire his 
very) remarkable tonal and technical 
achievements orto wonder at his ca- 
pacity for emotional excitement. ‘he 
present demand for seats for the Ko- 
cian-Kisner Concert, on this Saturday

THZ SATURDAY NEWS. -’ 


i Perrrirtrrririri ttt rtritit iri iti pt ii ttt tye 


in the Legislature this week, made the 
calculation that there are at least 385,- 
coo people in Alberta and that, with 
presen: prospects there should be no 
doub: whatever about che 400,009 
mark being turned by the time:of the 
next Dominion census in June, 191f. 
This is the figure at which the publici- 
ty department under his direction has 
been aiming and he and his asso- 
ciates do nat intend to take any 
chances but go ahead with a very ac- 
tive campaign all through the winter 
and spring. 

When it is considered that in 1906 
the province had but 180,000. people 
wud that in what is now Alberta there 
were only about. 70,000 in. 1901, such 
growth is, perhaps, the most remarka- 
ble that the continet has ver-scen. It is 
| particularly striking in view of° the 
| fact that we are only now beginning 
\to see railway development on a very 
large scale and withow: this we know 
how impossible it is for a country to 
realise its posibilites.

and a-good showing on this occasion 
will mean very much to the cause of 
development within the next decade. 
Strathcona property owners wouid 
benefit most of all. It is worth sum2- 
thing to be part of a city which has 
received the world-wide advertising 
that ‘has come to Edmonton. 


‘‘Wine was sent to make glad 
the heart of man.” 


To say that a property or a business 
enterprise is located near Edmonton 
or across the river from Edmonton is 
a very much different proposition to 
a man at a distance than if he is told 
that it is actually in Edmonton. 

I had a conversation during the past 
summer with a man whose business it 
s to handle securities and he told :ne 
of the difficuky that he had in explain- 
ing to customers the position of 
Strathcona. Anything with the name 
Edmonton attached appealed +o them 
immediately, but, though he drew dia- 
grams to show that Edmonton and 
Strathcona were to all intents and

Despite the cold weather. activity 

a: the imimgration hall and the land 
cffice continues in a most remarkable 
way. There can be no doubt that the 
L£00d year which all this part of the 
country had in 1910 has become well 
noised abroad and that we are to see 
4 very large movement in the spring. 
The situation from a general western 
standpoint is also satisfactory. Obed 
Emith, the Dominion commissioner at 
London, states that 30,000 Sccttish 
agricultural laborers will come out 
next year, while the London Outlook 
had this observation to make:— 

“The lates: statistics of Canadian 
immigration will be disquicting to any 


ea aoe ee 


one who has faith in a genuine revi- 
lval of British a griculiure. Scotland 

especially, is being drained of all the 
best elements in its rural population. 
Formerly the man who could not get 
on at home drifted to the-colonies in 
search of a situation. 
small farmer with 


Fine Scotch Whiskies 


Now it is the 
capital who is seiz- 
ing the wider opportunity for invest- 
ment offered by the Dominion.” 


From Ottawa comes the news of 
the formation of a $sm,c00 company 
to deal im real estate in all parts 14 
the Dominion. Headed by Mr. M. f 
O'Brien, the widely-known Renfrew 
mining operator, the board of dire:- 
tors includes R. M. Devlin of Ottawa, 
Hal McGiverin, M.P., the Ottawa Li- 
beral member in the House of Com- 
mons; Mr. John Gleason, a prominent 
citizen of Ottawa, and Mr. Frank S. 
Cahill of Saskatoon. 

An application for a charter for the 
company is now before Parliamen’

The real estate situation continues 
to show activity. A sale at $1,000 pet |o 
icot is reported on the south side of 
Jasper between Fourth and Fifth, ané 
a refusal of that figure on First Street 
in the first block off Jasper. Th 
Eiler block on McDougall, known ai 
McDougall court, has become the 
property of Dawson and Hyndman, 
at a price said to be in excess of $60,- 
ooo. Three large buildings are being 
projected for First Street. Robert 
Tegler intends to put up a six storey 
structure, which will house'a depart- 
mental store, on the south cast:corner 
of First and-Elizabeth, while: Crafts, 
Lee. and Gallinger propose to put ur 
a building that will cost in ithe neigh 
borhood of $200,000 on the northwes! 
corner of Peace. On the same side ot 
First and a ‘little closer to Jasper, 
Thomas Blackburn intends to build. 

F. C. Lowes -has purchased the 
South-eas: corner of First and Rice 


: Streets for: the sum of $50,000. The

eral sales on these sub-divisions dur- |

to have a number of others under way.

not.as: large‘as that of Winnipeg, -we 
must consider‘ the percentage. ~The 
totals for November,. 1909 and. 1919, 
respectively, in Vancouver were $32, 
572,215. and. $42,041,230. It ‘can be 
seen from this thatthe percentage of 
increase.in Vancouver is even greater 
than that of Winnipeg. 


“But wait till we see the case of Ed- 
monton. | The clearings there nearly 
snes the amounts for November 

this year. and November of last 
year being $82,026,620 and $4,422,818 
respectively. Just imagine that per- 
There are big things going 
on in this west of ours. Other west- 
ern cities also show splendid growth 
in this respect. The optimism of 
these. western provinces. is.not.mere 
‘boosting.” All anyone need do to be 
convinced is to look at the official 
figures, 


— 


A problem that is of considerable 
interest to cities with fast increasing 
real estate values, eventhough they 
have not the pretensions* of the 
American metropolis, has: been. dis- 
cussed a good deal of late in New 
York. Long term leases of real es- 
tate have not heretofore been pop 
lar there. They have been inexpe- 
dient for several reasons, the chiet 
of which is that when an owner sur- 
renders his property at a fixed uni- 


form rent he parts with the right of | 


benefitting by its increase in value, 
an increase which in a growing city 
is likely to be very great in the course 
of a long:pericd. At present, how- 
ever, owing to the size and cost of 
the modern types of buildings going 
up ‘in’ Manhattan, even wealthy 
ground owners :find it difficult to sup- 
ply the capital needed for improviug 


ket what they describe as an excep- their land. Prospeccrve builders, too, 


tionally attractive sub-division proper- 
tm. 


Full details will probably be avail- 
able in a week’s time. - | 


The Winnipeg Commercial has this 
to say regarding the growth of bank | 
clearings, with particular reference to 
that in Edmon:on: 

“One would imagine that the bank 
clearings this falll would have difficul- 


find difficulty in getting land by pur- 
|chase whén‘in’a large part of the 
berough sites are rarely appraised at 
less than $100,000. Hence long term 
leases are becoming a frequent ne- 
cessity. 

Not only have aie increased. in 
numbers of late, bu. a deal of inge- 
nuity has been-exercised in’ attempt- 
ing to arrive at am equitable distri- 
bution cf profits between owner and

“The total for Nov ember, 1909, was 
£116,569,351; this last November the 
total was $127,717,819. Bank clear- 
ings are recognised to be a good in- | 
dication of the business Situation, and 
we would ask our readers to note the 
Progress of these figures, if they do 
not realise the country’s progress. 
“There are even greater advances in 
other western cities than in those of 
Winnipeg. Take, for instance, the 
case of Vancouver, the brilliant coast 
city. The increase of the past Novem. 
ber over November, 1909, was abour 
$9,500,000, but while this increase is

Nothing succeeds like success a and nothing makes money like money 


—except real estate, 


A: well-chosen lot in Edmontorr may prove the 


stepping stone to undreamt of riches in the years to come. 


Edmonton real estate will bring you a bigger return than assy loan 
company, bank or commercial undertaking. Its value rests with every 


tick of the clock. 


Edmonton real estate is safe—it is the very best security in the 


world, 
steal it, 


It cannot run away—it cannot be destroyed and no man can 


It has been said that real estate is the foundation of wealth—we all 


know that money is the root of all evil, 


therefore you will do well to 


exchange your evil workitrg money .for the wealth winning real estate. 


If you are wise Edmonton real estate 


wealth. 


Buy Edmonton real estate noww—to-day. 


will be the 


Fortune 


foundation of your 


smiles upon the 


“man of to-day” but looks with angry frown upon the “man of to-mor- 


i row.”

be a good investme

The other night when Sarah Derr- 
hardt made her appearance in Chi- 
cago all, the papers there observeil 
that society was at the play. in 
commenting on tvis fact, the Cizavo 
Tribune remarks: 

“We are free to confess that we 
expected nothing less. We note it 
merely to call a:tzntion to the fact 
that Time, which has circled so kind- 
ly around «he wonderful Sarah, has 
dwelt generously with our community. 
Wrthout withering the charms of Ma: 
dame it has ripened us. 

* “It must be remembered that early 
in our acquaintance with Mme. Bern- 


MISS ALICE KENNEDY 
IN THE’ COLLEGE WIDOW. 


Monday Night, Empire Theatre. 


hard culture was a tender bud in the 
prospering communizy and our most 
careful gardeners were harried by the 


_ fear that it could not grow. When 


Eugene Field wrote his “Oon Criteck 


| 


contests do not exclude the profession- 
al singer or player entirely from 
Joming; on the contrary, companies 
numbering from’ tweaty-five to fify 
members may include four profession- 
al singers, or eight professional instru- 
mentalists, or both in the same pro- 
portion, whilst companies from sixty 
to one hundred members may contain 
ten per cent, professional singers or 
twenty per cent, instrumentalists, the 
cnly stipulation being that they be 
bona fide members of the club or com- 
pany competing and must in nowise be 
paid for their services in the perform- 
ance or the rehearsals for the same. 
Any companies smaller than the above 
mentioned may not include any pro- 
fessionals. | All professionals must be 
residents of the city or town from 
whence hails the entry It should in- 
terest all students to know that addi- 
tional competitions for male and fe- 
mele voices, piano and violin, will al- 
so be held, ant for which gold, silver, 
and bronze «mals ar: offered. \ 
special prize for the best original n.u- 
sical composition not ‘heretoiure 
published or produced will be offered. 
in the shape of $100, with a second 
prize of $50. 


Mr. Joseph Beecham, the father of 
the lessee of Covent Gacfen, Landon. 
has been making seige rs iackable ‘te 
velations in America. ‘fe says: “My

Mme. Sarah, if she were interested 
enough to have tke morning prints 
translated for her, must have observ- 
ed, not without a pardonable pride— 
she having been an instrument in our 
successful cultivation of the cultura 
and—the marked difference between 
this present itme and that of the “Cri- 
teek.” In the rap. eager audience 
which at her teet there was none 
with whem an argument might have 
been. started as to whether a line con- 
tained a “bong mo” or a “judy spree.” 
The uncertainty, the harrowing doubt 
of those early years, has been resoly- 
ed into the pleasant positiveness of a 
secure imelligence. 

I¢ will be remembered that in the 
“Criteck” was presented the picture 
of one of our prominent linseed in2r- 
chants in a wager with a merchant 
prince in the packing business, the 
point at hazard being the name of the 
play in which Madame appeared. Our 
linseed merchant risked $100 on his 
his belief that it was “Feodora,” the 
merchant prince put up to “Camecl.’ 
i awe

petitions in the west for the first time 
next spring has stimulated great inter- 
est all through the prairie provinces, 
and enquiries for information in nes- 
pect to them are coming in. They 
are divided into the following classes: 
(a) choral societies, (b) opera com- 
panics, It must be tinderstood that the

JACK WESTERMAN 
IN THE COLLEGE WIDOW, 
Monday Night at the Empire Theatre, 


son’s season of Opera Comique at 
His Majesty’s Theatre last summer re 
sulted in a loss of about £40,000. Ii 
every cat had been sold cut at every 
performance, the deficit would. still 
have been very large. The average 
loss on each of his seasons is ccnsider- 
ably over £35,000. But I have not 
grudged ome shilling of the money. 
I am glad that my boy has undertaken 
to give the English people good grand 
opera at popular prices. His un- 
questioned artistic sticazss has given 
me so much pleasure that I am fully 
repaid for the money that has been ex- 
pended. Some people spend their 
money on horse-racing. I prefer to 
devote mine to the catise of gran 
opera.” This is the utterance of a 
sportsman. But the Beechams are 
not alone in the field. I have just 
been watching the navvies digging 
‘oundations for the London Opera 
House which Mr. Hammerstein is 
House which Mr. Hamnterststa 
is building in Kingsway. Re- 
ferring to this, Mr. Beecham, se- 
nior, asserts that grand opera will 
never pay in London except at Covent 
Garden, becattse the Society element 
will not go elsewhere. He sympathises 
with Mr. Hammerstein, who, true to 
his name, will soon make the air ring 
with his chisels—T.P.’s Weekly. 


(Continued on page eight 


HEAR HARRY LAUDER SING 
HIS INIMITABLE SCOTTISH 
SONGS AT HARMONY 

HALL. * 


— 


Owing to Harry Lauder’s existiny 
contracts in the Old Country, he wil! 
not be able ¢o fill an Edmonton en- 
gagement for another year. How- 
ever, the many thousands of Fis ad 
mirers can, meantime, have the sa‘- 
isfaction of hearing him at Harmoay 
Hall, in his Scottish and humorous 
songs, on the Magnificent Victrola. 

It is said that to hear Lauder’s 
songs on the Victrola is even better 
than hearing him personally, as ont 
cathches so clearly his Scottis’ ac- 
cént and ‘that inimitable bursrer which 
is Harry Lauder’s specialty. 

Visitors to Harmony Hall, the Geo. 
H. Suckling Piano Warerooms, 
Rice Street, will be cordially welcome. 


SCENE FROM THE TRAVELLING SALESMAN 


To be Presented Saturday Night at 


- PA'S FAILING FACULTIES. 


Pa paid six dollars for a hat; 
thinks he’s lost his mind; 

It’s lined with silk, and one of those 
brown, softish, woolly kind; 

He brought it honge the other night 
and. kept it on a while; 

Before she found owt what it cost ma 
kind of liked its style; 

But when pa told her what he paid she 
sank down ina chair, 

And said: “Pa, every day you get more 
foolish, I declare. 


ma 


shouldn't think,” she said, “that 
you would waste your cash like 
that! 
Nobody but a fool would pay six dol- 
lars for a hat! 
The ohildren meed new overcoats; 
their shoes are shabby, too; 
Sometimes you make it hard for me to 
keep respectin’ you; 
Think of the many ways I try to save 
day after day; 
And here you go and waste your cash 
in such a foolish way. 


“y 


“If we had millions, 1 should be the 
last one to complain. 


137-9 , But here we live from hand to mouth 


—it's just a constant strain! 


the Empire Theatre. 


1 must deny myself the things I often 
wish I thad. 

And then you buy a hat like that —It 
simply is too bad! 

Six dollars merely thrown away! Jt 
fills nie with the blues— 

Six dollars for a hat. 

two pairs of shoes 


Enough to buy 


4 sat and listened for a while as 
though he felt ashamed, , 
And pretty soon he says: “Well, I'll 
admit I should be blamed; 
Six dollars for a hat’s too much; I'll 
take it back—but say, 
How much did that one cos: that you 
brought home the other day?” 
Ma left the room and we could see a 
tear roll down her cheek; 
She's worried over pa and thinks his 
mind is gettin’ weak 
Chicago Record Herald. 
a en rene 


It is not easy to see how the coun- 
try will benefit by the payment of 
members of parliament. But the 
proposition is almost. certain to -go 
through, Avecause, while it is to the 
edvantage of many people to sup- 
port it, it is not the business of any- 
body in particular to oppose it —

THUNDER AND LIGHTNING. 


| A little fellow listened open-mout!:- 
lto the description of a railroad acci- 


A bishop came to visit a church | dent in which a man was very serious- 


where a colored minister was presid-| |) injured. When one of the family 
ing. Loudly and with much gesticu-| remarked, “I think, he could get dam- 
Jation the preacher proclaimed Salva-jages from the railroads,” the litsle 
tion. When he had finished, he ap- | | chap was puzzled and broke out with, 


proached the bishop and asked how | “But father, hasn’t he got damages 
he liked the sermon. 

The bishop answered: ‘Why, pretty 
well; but don’t you thing you spoke: 
too loud?” 

“Wel,” said the pre acher, “It’s this | 
way: what I lacks in lightning I tries | 
to make up in thunder.” a) 


ijenough now?” 


Seli-conquest lies in obedience, obe- 
| dience lies in knowledge.—Clodd. 


It is a great thing to be satisfie 
with what we have.—Edward Bok

the heart of man.

THE INVESTOR 


must consider ‘the percentage.’ The 


| totals for November, 1909 and: 1919, 
%| respectively, in Vancouver. were $32, 


572,215 and $42,041,230. It can be 


S xeRA DORE SERERODET ER SORE RERRERESERDD EEDREREREEEESD ET seen from this that the percentage of 


Hon. Duncan Marshall, in speaking 
in the Legislature this week, made the 
calculation that there are at least 385.- 
coo people in Alberta and that, with 
presen: prospects there should be no 
doubt whatever about «he - 400,009 
mark being turned by the time: of the 
next Dominion census in June, 191f. 
This is the figure at which the publici- 


ty department under his direction has | 


been aiming and he and his asso- 
ciates do not intend to. take. any 
chances but go ahead with a very ac- 
tive campaign all through the winter 
and spring. ; 

When it is considered that in 1906 
the province had but 180,000- people 
S that in what is now Alberta there 
‘were only about. 70,000 in. 1901, such 
growth is, perhaps, the most remarka- 
ble that the continet has ver-scen. It is 
| particularly striking in view of) the 
| fact that we are only now beginning 
to see railway development on a very 
large scale and without this we know 
how impossible it is for a country to 
realise its posibilites. 


The Mayor of Edmonton estmated 
the other day that the city contained j 
at the present time at least 30,000, peo- + 
ple. Those-who have been in a post- 


ee 2 en 


tion to form an opinion, men like the 


eiectric light superintendent and the 
heads of other city departments sup- 
plying different services, fully. bear 
out what the mayor says: Nor’ can 
one play the part of merely.a casual 
observer and walk and drive about 
without realising how rapid has been 
the influx. The decision of the 
Strathcona ratepayers on Monday 
when a council was returned that js 
favorable to taking up the question of 
amalgamation means much. All. those 
who have property in the two cities 
should exert themselves to have this 
result brought about before the cen- 
sus next June, for if the greater city 
couldhen show a population of well 
up to 40,000, where in 1901 Edmonton 
itself had only 2,700 and in 1906, 11.- 
coo, the effect . would: be, excellent. 
There is nothing succeeds like succese 
and a:good showing on this occasion 
will mean very much to the cause of 
development within the next decade. 
Strathcona property owners wouid 
benefit most of all. It is worth sum2- 
thing to be part of a city which has 
received the world-wide adverti 
that ‘thas come to Edmonton. 


To say that a property or a business 


a very much different proposition to 
a man at a distance than if he is told 
that it is actually in Edmonton. 

I had a conversation during the past 
summer with a man whose business it 
is to handle securities and he told me 
of the difficukty that he had in explain- 
ing to customers the position of 
Strathcona. Anything with the name 
Edmonton attached appealed to them 
immediately, but, though he drew dia- 
grams to show shat Edmonton and 
Strathcona were to all intents and 
purposes one place and were bound 
to become one place in actuality be- 
fore long, they fought shy of the 
smaller city. 


Despite the cold weather, activity 

: the imimgration hall and the land 
cffice continues in a most remarkable 
way. There can be no doubt that the 
ood year which all this part of ¢he 
country had in 1910 has become well 
noised abroad and that we ate to see 
a very large movement in the spring. 
The situation from a general western 
Standpoint is also satisfactory. Obed 
Emith, the Dominion commissioner at 
London, states that 30,000 Sccttish 
agricultural laborers will come out 
next year, while the London Outlook 
had this observation to make:— 

“The latest statistics of Canadian 
immigration will be disquieting to any 
one who has faith in a genuine revi- 
val of British agriculture. Scotland | 
especially, is being drained of all the 
best elements in its rural population. 
Formerly the man who could not get 
on at home drifted to the-colonies in 
search of a situation. 


Now it is the | 
small farmer with capital who is seiz- 
ing the wider opportunity for invest- 
ment offered by the Dominion.” 


From Ottawa comes the news of 
the formation of a $s00,coo company 
to deal im real estate in all parte «1 
the Dominion. Headed by Mr. M. f 
O'Brien, the widely-known Renfrew 
mining operator, the board of dire-:- 
tors includes R. M. Deviin of Ottawa, 
Hal McGiverin, M.P., the Ottawa Li- 
beral member in the House of Com- 
mons; Mr. John Gleason, a prominent 
citizen of Ottawa, and Mr. Frank S. 
Cahill of Saskatoon. 

An application for a charter for the 
company is now before Parliameny 


eee eee eae eene eee ae 


enterprise is located near Edmonton $116 
or across the river from Edmonton is 


increase ‘in Vancouver is even greater 


and t is expected to have everything than that of Winnipeg. 


ready to. begin operations «within 4 
few months. 


The real estate situation continues 
to show activity. 
icot is reported on the south side 9? 
Jasper between Fourth and Fifth, ané 
a refusal of that figure on First Strect 
in the first block off Jasper. Tha 
Eiler block on McDougall, known ai 
McDougall court, has ‘become .the 
property of Dawson and Hyndman, 
at a price said to be in excess of S60,- 
eoo. Three large buildings are being 
projected for First Street. Robert 
Tegler intends to put up a six storey 
structure, which will house ’a depart- 
mental store,.on the south cast:corner 
of First and Elizabeth, while: Crafts, 
Lee. and Gallinger propose to put up 
a building that will .cost in the neigh 
borhood of $200,000 on the northwest 
corner of Peace. On the same side ot 
First and alittle closer to Jasper, 
Thomas Blackburn intends to build. 


—— 


F.C. Lowes..has purchased the 
South-eas: corner of First and Rice 
Streets forthe sum of $50,000. The 
: ‘property has a frontage of 6o0it. on’ 
iFirst Street and 78 ft. on Rice Street. 

Interest on the Groat Estate and In- 
glowood is growing rapidly. F.C. 
Lowes and Co. have put through sev- 
eral sales on these sub-divisions dur- 
ing the past ten-days and are reported 
to have a number of others under way. 
This firm is about to place on-the mar- 

‘et what they describe as an excep- 
tionally attractive sub-division proper- 
ty 

Full details will probably be av ail- 
able in a week’s time. 


The Winnipeg Commercial has this 


to say regarding the growth of bank | 


clearings, with particular reference to 
that in Edmonton: 

“One would imagine that the bank 
clearings this falll would have difficul- 
ty in reaching the figures of a year 
ago, since this'year we have had such 
a hue and cry about poor crops. Re- 
sults talk, however, and it is gratify- 

jing to note that the Winnipeg’ bank 
clearings for November. just passe 
were eleven million dollars in advance 
of those for November of last year. 

“Ttsis “not so much that $11,000,009 
is so great, it-is when we look at the 


ising percentage, and also at the fact that 


so many did not look for such encour- 
agement. 

“The total for November, 1909, was 
569,351; this last November the 
total was $127,717,819. Bank clear- 


ings are recognised to be a good in-| 


dication of the business Situation, and 
we would ask our readers to note the 
Progress of these figures, if they do 
not realise the country’s progress. 
“There are even greater advances in 
other western cities than in those of 
Winnipeg. Take, for instance, the 
case of Vancouver, the brilliant coast 
city. The increase of the past Novem- 
ber over November, 1909, was abour 
$9,500,000, but while this increase is 


“ceimage. 


“But wait till we see the case of Ed- 
nionton, The clearings there nearly 
doubled, the amounts for November 
of this year and November of last 


A sale at $1,000 P| ear being $82,026,620 and $4,422,818 


respectively. Just imagine that per- 
There are big things going 
on in this west of ours. Other west- 
ern cities also show splendid growth 
in this respect. The optimism of 
these western provinces.is..not.mere 
‘boosting.” _ All anyone need do to be 
convinced is to look at the official 
figures. 


A problem that is of considerable 
interest to cities with fast increasing 
real estate values, even though they 
have not the pretensions® of the 
American metropolis, has~ been: dis- 
cussed a good deal of late in New 
York. Long term leases of real es- 
tate have not heretofore. been pop 
ular there. They have been inexpe- 
dient for several reasons, the chiet 
of which is that when an owner sur- 
renders his property at a fixed uni- 


form rent he parts with the right of} 


benefitting by its increase in value, 
an increase which in a growing city 
is likely to be very great in the course 
of a long -pericd. _At present, how- 
‘ever, owing to the size and cost of 
the modern types of buildings going 
tp (in). Manhattan, even wealthy 
ground owners: find it difficult to sup- 
ply the capital needed for improviug 
their land. Prospecsrve builders, too, 
find difficulty in getting land by pur- 
jchase whén“in*a large part of the 
| borough sites are rarely appraised at 
tiess than $100,000. Hence Iceng term 
Icases are becoming a frequent ne- 
cessity. 

Not only have they increased in 
numbers of late, bu. a deal of inge- 
ruity has been-exercised in’ attempt- 
ing to arrive..at.an: equitable distri- 
bution of profits between owner and 
lessez.’ The most novel device is a 
provision that the rent shall be a cer- 
tzin percentage of the lessee’s: gross 
income! from the business which hz 
conducts on the premises. 
common agreememt calls: for a read- 
justment of the’ rent at short inter 
vals. With a fair division of profits 
the tendency is encouraged of ex-! 
tending the*¢erm of the lease. The

will) be about $20,000,000. 


The dime is | undoubtedly fairly 
near, we are told by a-good authori- 
ty, when freehold transfers will be 
of infrequent occurrence in’ Manhat- 
tan, 
apantment ‘houses represent with 
their sites too much wealth to be 
owned permanently and as a rule by 
individuals. Indeed, corporate + ow- 


extent. The Rhineland Real Estate 
Company, with a_capitalisation of $6,- 
500,000, is an illustration of the meth- 
od by which the heirs to big landed 
estates nowadays prefer to take over. 
their interests. Of ordinary. invest- 
ment and operating realty corpora- 
tions there are said to be not fewer 
than 8,000 with offices in this bor- 
ough, although probably “a good 
share of their;holdings is — outside 
Manhattan. Under. the twofold in- 
fluence ‘of corporate., ownership . and 
long term leases property rights in 
real estate will befar more widely 
distributed than has been «he = case 
heretofore. :

Nothing succeeds like success aand nothing makes money like money 


—except real estate, 


A well-chosen lot in Edmonton may prove the 


stepping stone to undreamt of riches in the Years to come. 


Edmonton real estate will bring you a bigger return than any loan 


company, bank or 
tick of the clock. 


commercial undertaking. 


Its value rests with every 


Edmonton teal estate is safe—it is the very best security in the 


world. 
steal it. 


It cannot run away—it cannot be destroyed and no man can 


It has been said that real estate is the foundation of wealth—we all 
know that money is the root of all evil, therefore you will do well to 
exchange your evil workig money.for the wealth winning real estate. 


If you are wise Edmonton real estate 


wealth. 


will be the 


foundation of your 


Buy Edmonton real estate noww—to-day, Fortune smiles upor the 
“man of to-day” but looks with angry frown upon the “man of to-mor- 


i row.” 


One word more—don’t you think a lot in the Groat Estate would, 


be a good investment?

Music and Drama 


~The other night when Sarah Derr- 
hardt made-‘her appearance in Chi- 
cago all. the papers cher: observeil 
that..society was at the play. tn 
commenting on tzis fact, the Ciicavo 
Tribune remarks: 

“We are fnee to confess that we 
expected nothing less. We note it 
merely to call a:tzntion to the fact 
that Time, which has circled so kind- 
lv. around the wonderful Sarah, has 
dwelt generously with our community. 
Wrhout withering the charms of Ma- 
dame it has ripened us. 

* “Tt must be remembered that early 
in our acquaintance with Mme. Bern- 


MISS ALICE KENNEDY 
IN THE COLLEGE WIDOW. 


Monday Night, Empire Theatre. 


hard: culture was a tender bud in the 
prospering communi:y and our most 
careful gardeners were harried by the 
. fear that. it could not grow. When 
Eugene Field wrote his “Oon Criteck 
de Bernhardt,” it was apparent. that 
the delicate roots were reaching tim- 
idly into the ‘soil and that with cara 
flourishing. bloom might be expesoted. 

Mme. Sarah, if she were interested 
enough to have the morning prints 
translated for her, must have observ- 
ed, not without a pardonable pride— 
she having been an instrument in our 
successful cultivation of the cultura 
and—the marked difference betwen 
this present itme and tha: of the “Cri- 
teeck.” 5 In the rapt. eager audience 
which at*her tees there was none 
with whom an argument migh: have 
been. started as to whether a line con- 
tained a “bong mo” or a “judy spree.” 
The uncertainty, the harrowing doubt 
of those early years, has been resolv- 
ed into the picasant positiveness of a 
secure intelligence. 

I¢ will be remembered that in the 
“Criteck” was presented the picture 
of.one of our prominent linseed iner- 
chants in a wager with a merchant 
prince in the packing business, the 
point at hazard being the name of the 
playin which Madame appeared. Our 
linseed merchant risked $100 on his 
his belief that it was “Feodora,” the 
merchant prince put up to “Cameel.’ 

The holding of the Earl Grey com- 
petitions in the west for the first time 
next spring has stimulated great inter- 
est all through the prairie provinces, 
and enquiries for information in nes- 
pect to them are coming in. They 
are divided into the following classes: 
(a) choral societies, (b) opera com- 
panics, It must be understood that the

SATURDAY 
in only one 


GRAND CONCERT 

SEPARATE SCHOOL HALL 
* supported by HerrMaurice Hisner 

the distinguished German Pianist 


atid others. . 
€  * geats $1 and 82, Plan now-open 
atHermony Hall the Geo, H. 
Suckling Warerooms for the 
Pianos of Nordhelmer, Stein. 
ways and Sons, Lansdowne and 


Haines Bros, 


} 


contests do not exclude the profession- 
et singer or player entirely from 
Joming; on the contrary, companies 
numbering from’ twenty-five to filsy 
members may include four profession- 
al Singers, or eight professional instru- 
mentalists, or both in the same pro- 
portion, whilst companies from sixty 
to one hundred members may contain 
ten per cent, professional singers or 
twenty per cent, instrumentalists, the 
cnly stipulation being that they be 
bona fide members of the club or com- 
pany competing and must in nowise be 
paid for their services in the perform- 
ance or the rehearsals for the same. 
Any companies smaller than the above 
mentioned may not include any pro- 
fessionals. All. professionals must be 
residents of the city or town from 
whence hails the entry It should in- 
terest all students to know that addi- 
tional competitions for male and fe- 
mele voices, piano and violin, will al- 
so be held, ant for which gold, silver, 
and bronze m:dals ars offered. \ 
special prize for the best original nur- 
sical composition not ‘heretofure 
published or produced will be offere:t. 
in the shape of $100, with a second 
prize of $50. 


Mr. Joseph Beecham, the father of 
the lessee of Covent Garden, London. 
has been making some retwarkable te- 
velations in,America. He says: “My 


JACK WESTERMAN 
IN THE COLLEGE WIDOW, 
Monday Night at the Empire Theatre, 


son’s season of Opera Comique at 
His Majesty's Theatre last summer re: 
sulted in a loss of about £40,000. li 
every iat had been sold cut at every 
performance, the deficit, would. still 
have been very large. The average 
loss on each of his seasons is consider- 
ably over £35,000. But I have not 
gcrudged one shilling of the money. 
I am glad that my boy has undertaken 
to give the English people good grand 
opera at popular prices. His un- 
questioned artistic sucozss has given 
me so much pleasure that I am fully 
repaid for the money that has been ex- 
pended. Some people spend their 
money on horse-racing. I prefer to 
devote mine to the cause of grand 
cpera.” ‘This is the utterance of a 
sportsman. But the Beechams are 
not alone in the field. I have just 
been watching the navvies digging 
:ovndations for the London Opera 
House which Mr. Hammerstein is 
House which Mr. Hammerststa 
is building in Kingsway.  Re- 
ferring to this, Mr. Beecham, se- 
nior, asserts that grand opera will 
never pay in London except at Covent 
Garden, because the Society element 
will not go elsewhere. He sympathises 
with Mr. Hatnmerstcin, who, true to 
his name, will soon make. the air ring 
with his chisels—T.P.’s Weekly. 


(Continued on page eight 


HEAR HARRY LAUDER SING 
HIS INIMITABLE SCOTTISH 
SONGS AT HARMONY 
HALL. ° 


Owing to Harry Lauder's existins 
contracts in the Old Country, he will 
not be able to fill an Edmonton en- 
gagement for another year. How- 
ever, the many thousands of his ad 
mirers can, meantime, have the sa‘- 
isfaction of hearing him at Harmoay 
Hall, in his Scottish and humorous 
songs, on the Magnificent Victrola. 

It is said that to hear Latder’: 
songs on the Victrola is even better 
than hearing him. personally, as ont 
cathches so clearly his Scottish ac- 
cént and ‘that inimitable burerr which 
is Harry Lauder’s specialty. 

Visitors to Harmony Hall, the Geo. 
H. Suckling Piano Warerooms, 137-9, 
Rice Street, will be cordially welcome. |

Floral Gifts 


There is always a scarcity of flowers at Christmas. 


This year-all florists eXpect greater shortage than ever owing to lack of sunshine and increased demand 


By refusing all wholesale orders from out side points we expect to be able to supply our local demand. 


We have thousands of the Lily of the Valley, Paper white Narcissius and Roman Hyacinthe which will 


-be in excellent condition 


next week. 


Our Carnations are headed with bloom. 


~*~ Hundreds of Azaleas, Ferns and Primulas. 


Finest English Holly and Mistletoe 


Our Greenhouses are open in the evenings, giving you an opportunity 


your selection. 


Our prices are very

SCENE FROM THE TRAVELLING SALESMAN 


To be Presented Saturday Night at 


« PA’S FAILING FACULTIES. 


Pa paid six dollars for a hat; ma 
thinks he’s lost his mind; 

It’s lined with silk, and one of those 
brown, softish, woolly kind; 

He brought it honge the other night 
and_kept it on a while; - 
Before she found ott what it cost ma 

kind of liked its style; 
But when pa told her what he paid she 
sank down ina chair, 
And said: “Pa, every day you get more 
* foolish, I declare, 


shouldn’t think,” she said, “that 
you would waste your cash like 
that! 
Nobody but a fool would pay six dol- 
lars for a hat! 
The children need new overcoats; 
their shoes are shabby, too; 
Sometimes you make it hard for me to 
keep respectin’ you; 
Think of the many ways I try to save 
day after day; 
And here you go and waste your cash 
in such a foolish way. 


“y 


“1¢ we had millions, 1 should be the 
- last one to complain. 
But here we live from hand to mouth 
it’s just a constant strain! 


OE 


the Empire Theatre. 


I must deny nryself the things I often 
wish I thad. 

And then you buy a hat like that —It 
simply is too bad! 

Six dollars merely thrown away! It 
fills neswith the blues— 

Six dollars for a hat. Enough to buy 

two pairs of shoes!" 

sat and listened for a while as 
though he felt ashamed, , 

And pretty soon he says: “Well, 1'll 
admit 1 should be blamed; 

Six dollars for a hat’s too much; II 

take it back—but say, 


Pa 


How much did that one cos: that you 
brought home the other day?” 
Ma left the room and we could see a 
tear roll down her check; 
She’s worried over pa and thinks his 
mind is gettin’ weak 
Chicago Record Herald. 
en ly 


It is not easy to see how the coun- 
try will benefit by the payment of 
members of parlianvent. But the 
proposition is.almost. certain to-go 
through, Aecause, while it is to the 
edvantae of many people to sup- 
port it, it is not the business of any- 
body in particular to oppose it.—-

MUSIC AND DRAMA. 


(Continued from pzge seven.) 


? 


Since this department was written 
last week, Dr. Harriss of Ottawa, 
whose enterprise was referred to then, 
has visited Edmonton.and succeeded 
in awakening not a little intercst in 
his project. The members of the 
Board of Trade have agreed to help 
it along by arranging a suitable recep- 
tion to the visitors. As Dr. Harriss 
explained to them, a large propontion 
of the men comprising the Sheffield 
choir are business men, whose object 
in taking the trip so far away from 
home is not wholly an artistic one. 
They are enthusiastic musicians but 
are glad of the opportunities which 
they receive in this way to enlarge 
their knowledge of commercial con- |

And shows. that. cost one-and—a-haii; 

Some made me weep, some'made me 
laugh. 

I’ve seen the villain’ slammed in jail 

Without a friend to go his baz], 

And when the act was done, somehow 

Come out of jail and make his bow! 


I’ve seen the pale young heroine 
Who had escaped the lures of sin 
And saved her poor-old father’s hide 
While all were watching, tearful- 
eyed— 

I've seen her leap from a high cliff 
And heard the bass drum go ker-biff! 
Then up the curtain came, and she 
Rowed calmly there for al! to see. 


I’ve seen the hero say farewell, 
And stop a moment just to tell 
How far away he meant to go

One of the receptions too late to 
chronicle last week, was Mrs. Murray 
Miller’s on Thursday afternoon, when 
Mrs. Miller received for the first time, 
since moving here from Lloydminster. 

The rooms were prettily decorated 
with quantities of cut flowers, the tea- 
table, where Mrs. Cornwall, Mrs. Dun- 
can Marshall, and Mrs, #rank Walker 
presided, being done with pink roses 
and carnations on an exquisite lace 
tea-cloth. 

Mrs. Miller received 1 a becoming 
sown of dark green chiffon ower-green 
satin, and looked exceedingly smar‘. 


Mrs. Duncan Marshal was the hos:- 
ess of a jolly little luncheon of eight 
covers on Wednesday, the guests be- 
ing, Mrs. Sifton, Mrs. C. H. Mitchell, 
Mrs. Jennings, Mrs. Howard Douglas, 
Mrs. Patterson, Mrs. MacArthur, Miss 


es | McIsaacs and Miss Patterson. 


The table was a picture with a great 
shower of pink roses in the centre, 
and Mrs. Marshall wore a modisa 
blouse of green fish met over white 
satin, with a trig tailored skirt. 


Mr. and Mrs. J. K. Cornwall en- 
tertained a box party for “The Liars,” 
afterwards giving a vety smart sup- 
Per party at the King Edward Grill, 
the guests including Mr. and Mrs. 
Lowes of Calagry. 


Mrs. Charlesworth has issued cards 
for an “At Home” on Monday, Dec- 
ember 19th. 


On Tuesday evening the mamhers 
of the North Star Chapte- No. wt cf 
the A.F. and A.M. vecre At Hone 
to their friends and a most enjoyable 
evening was spent. The installation 
cf officers was followed by a concert 
in the lodge rooms, in the course of 
whick an addres. wat given by Grand 
Lecturer Nixon. Adjournment was 
then made to the Sepazte School Hail, 
where a dance was heFd, Tirrner’s Or- 
chestra supplying excellent music. The 
officers of the lodge dre George 3%. 
McLeod, first principal, Charles Me- 
Donaldfi second princzpal, F. S. Wat- 
son, third principal, J. J. Dunlop, 
scribe E, Dr. Darling, scribe N,

Christmas Store

as these, but no other store, we. are sure, 
will make them right in the heart of the sea-— 
son. Not~all of the holiday sale bargains are. 
mentioned to-day, but enough to convince you 
that it is a money-saving opportunity well 
worth taking advantage of. ITS TO YOUR.

It is customary to hold clearing sales in J. anuary and to radically alice the prices of 
winter garments for such an occasion. But we are not in the habit of doing custom-. 
, ary things. We would rathér hold a sale that gives you the reduction now, instead of 
next January—and that’s what we shall do tomorrow. | This sale gives you the gar- 
ments a month ahead or time, so that you can get the benifit of them during the cold 
weather of December.

great statemen or generals, and if we 
had to how many is the chance likely 
to come to display them. ‘To pay tri- 
bute to their achievements js all very 
well in its way, but what does it ac- 
complish in the way of inducing oth- 
ers to follow their examples? Is it 
not better to devote our attention to 
showing some cutward mark of our 
appreciation of the virtues of those 
found in the humbler walks of life in 
close touch with our own?

not right that it should be so. 


“The Love of the Wild,” “The Dop 
Doctor,’ “Handicap,” and “Mistress 
of Shenstone,” ate among the best 
novels this year. Little has ’em. 


It is a standitig disgrace to the peo- 
ple of Edmonton and Strathcona that 
they have done nothing to honor the 
memory of George Lamb, the mine’ 
superintendent who, four years ago, 
gave his life in 80 heroic a manner, in 
the vain attempt to save the men un- 


(Continued frow: 


The memory of a man like Alder- 
son cannot be paid too much honor 
to. To do so is to stimulate others to 
prepare themselves for rising to emer- 
gencies in the way that he did. Few 


pursuit of what he considecs 
simple duty.

majority of those who lived here then

planting altogether wrong ideats in 

the minds of the rising generation. 
‘| One of the greatest poems ever writ- 
ten is Colonel John Hay’s “Wreck of 
the Prairie Bell,” not because it: mea- 
sures up fo the ordinary canons of 
literary perfection, but because. in 
Jim Bludso he places on the pedestal 
every man who does the heroic thinz, 
with no trumpéts blowing and in the

Mr. S. M. Crothers, in the ‘Atlantic 
Monthly,’ has. avery, vivacious. paper 
“On Liking One’s Job,” in which he 
“throws. some light; on tthe problem of 
what’ makes’ work. a pleasure, ‘and 


% 


‘bute. 


Though Working 


Lp ees os ~ ee 


Nevertheless ‘his natural af- 
finity for gypsies, muleteers, and pic- 
turesque ;vagabonds | of: all .sorts en- 
abled’ him ¢o carry the Bible into out- 


-denics that it is necessary to be miser- | Of-the-way places’ which would never 
able in doing one’s work, or even in| have. been.dreamed of. by. a: zealous 


-doing good! 


“Among the most persistent of my|: 


-early, dreams was that of being a mis- 
sionary,” says, Mr. Crothers.. “I 
wanted to be a missionary .before it 
occurred t6é: mie that’ I had’ any” par- 
» ticular doctrine to. communicate or 
‘manner of life to recommend. Indeed 


I now,perceiyve that my call: was more | 


-of Nature than of Grace. 
“IT wanted to be a-missionary bécause 

I Jonged: to-.go'.on missionary. jour- 

cneys. The call of the wild, the lure 
-of” the anknown, the fascination of 
terrestrial mystery, takes many forms. 
_ It isiall:a part of the romance of. g 0- 
“grapliy, which has survived’ even. the 
“invention, of maps. ; 
~ “When. one is-eleven and going on 


‘to go to the Antipodes, to visit No 
“Man’s Land; to wander throtigh for-. 
‘saken cities, to climb lonely. towers, 

:and to look out through 
‘Magic casements opening oh 
foaty pe? 


ot 


OF perilous seas oe” 

“In differentgénerations this de- 
‘mand hasbeen variously. met. The 
instifetions of civilization, besides 


Hie 


secondary function of satisfying .the 
youthful desire to-go into a far. coun- 
‘try, a desire not of the prodigal alone. 


thas its fingerpost:.pointing to the un- 
known.” a igh enter heath 2 
Was Grandmother Right? 

When the writer of. the article 
..4,,missionary_ because. 

one who “had cressed 
deserts. in caravanS and had endured 
perils of robbers;” when’ the. fell. in 
Jove with Persia. and ‘read:.Thomas 
Moore’s ‘Lalla Rookh, and the ‘Ara- 
bian. Nights,’ and was’ saturated: with 
tthe glow and. color of the: East, his 
-grandmother ‘checked’ him, for she 
‘said: ‘You must not go as a mission- 


‘:ary to Persia, for if you do the Per- 


-sians will convert you.” ~ 

Now he asks, “Was-my grandmother 
‘right in thinking. that my pleasure, in 
Persia was likely to be a detriment 
‘to. my usefulness? Was I less likely 
to do good tto the, Persians because J 
‘thought well of them to begin with? 
And would it have been a waste’ f 
-time if, after a term of years, I had 
-partly converted the Persians and the. 
Persians had partly converted me? 
May there not:be a profitable-recipro- 


_ -eity in spiritual ‘influence? 


“In attempting to answer . such 
-questions we encounter the prejudic: 
-which exists among the more moral 
-and intellectual classes against mixed 
-motives. There seems something e- 
specially meritorious in’ thé more un- 
-pleasing manifestations of. duty, as 
then we are free from:any doubts as 
-to its being the genuine article: If 
the duty happens not to be disagree 
cable, we try to make it appear so. 
Thus, a ‘pattiotic’ ‘citizen, being “ttoni- 
-inated-for.an. office of dignity, is care- 
‘fal to inform his‘coristituents that tre 

“A disciple came’ to Confucius, 
and with that admirable directness in 
-asking questions characteristic of 
Chinamen, enquired, ‘Master, are you 
a sage?’ Coifucitis’ answeted; ‘No, I 
am not a sage}, 1 am only one who 
‘earns without” satlety, and who 
teaches without getting tired.’ 

“There was John Wesley. _ His 
~‘Journal? with its record of indefati- 
gable labor, is one of the cheeriest 
“Hooks in the langiiage” What a tare 
good time he had! When he. was 

eighty-seven he could say, ‘ I do not 
‘remember to have felt lowness af 
spirits for a quarter of an hour since 
{ was born’ For mone than sixty 
-years this indefatigable pleasure] 
seeker had been doing as he pleased. 
“Up every day in time’ to preach’ at 
5 o'clock in. the mofning; then over 
-the hills or through the pleasant 
lanes to preach again at abowt the 
time lazy citiens were ready for 
breakfast; off again, om horseback or 
“by chaisé of in’a lumbering ‘stage- 
coach, for more preaching to vast 
cfowds of sinners—just the kind of 
sirinets he liked to preach to. Be- 
-wkta séfmons he refrestied his mind 
with all” sorts * aftd ~ conditions of 
~ books, ; AES SN 
«When we réad Bottow’s “Bible in 

Spain,! we’ feel that Borrow would 
“have gone td Spaiti atiy way, even if 
-there had béén''no Bibles to distri- 


SS Se eS 


person of, sedentary. habits. 
“There’is: always: something. to be 
said in favor of ‘the strong’ bent’ of 
the. -spirit, whether it tends. toward 
Connecticut or: Persia.” ;

THE WESTERNER, WHO HAS|"Jo get out of itt wood, 


YET TO SUCCEED. 


I have been in’ Old’ Kentucky, 
And I’ve been in Tennessee, 
And I’ve travelled over half the 
globe by’ now— ; 
In the strange alluring climates 
Of the lands beyond the sea, 


But it’s over and I’ve settled— 
made a vow, 


I've vowed I'll cut out roving, 
That the blessed life don’t pay, 
That it makes you discontented, 
gives tio rest; 
And I'm here in Western Canada 
‘No take part in the fray, 
And I’m going to make her hand 
me out the best. 


The lucky ones have made their pile, 
The careful ones are rich, 
And half a million people seem to 

thrive; 

But I afd my associates 

Are always iti the ditch, 

* Just fighting hard ‘and struggling 
td ‘stitvive. 


I've worked at every blessed job, 
That I can find to do, 


And that I avedy 1 jobs turn ou 


the sation 


No tore of w vierl'may for 4 wage, 


That hardly psqpiyrg tele ter, 
And help): lo« =e thre 
ready tleizths; 
1M start of fF hr tmayself his time 
On me anid fittmtes, Z'm bert, 
And’ a tefo¢hizing- Erandl to 
who’teit lt thee Glitch. 


guys a: 


those 


It’s kill or wruué thei) tlme all right, 
Just purely diesit® cor swim,

for tit, .%,

And satisfy ut tl hlew,
