AGINCOURT. 


From the London Chronicle, 

Agincourt, from which Henry V. 
inate his tridmphal entry into London 
for the second time yesterday, is our 
English way of spelling Azincourt, 
just as Blenheim is a similar corrup~ 
tion of Blindheim, In abowt two and 
a half hours Henry’s little army slew 
101000 Frenchmen, neatly: twice as 
tany as we lost outright in battle dur- 
ing our Boer war, lasting over two 


and a thalf years—andyct we’ talk com- 
placently of our “modern arms of pre- 
cision’! A monument at Agincourt 
marks the cemetery of the slain. Few 
battlefields have changed their ap- 
pearance so little. The field may ba 
reached within a railway hour from 
Foulogne, but few tourists go to the 
scene of the most wonderful feat of 
arms of our race, 


A good man is he who does great 
and good things, though he risks 
everything by it.—Marius. 


. The wise man is never satisfied 
that he knows, but the foolish knows 
that he is satisfied —Prideaux. 


over bow of 


Sargent’s statements, woman's ultra- 
feminine characteristics—small wails, 
large hips, small feet, etc.—weno over- 


developed, and she was the positive 
antithesis of man in physical propor- 
tions, ‘I'oday her physique has be- 
come revolutionized, he says, and al- 
ready approximates that of the other 
sex, ‘his great evolution has taken 
place, says Dr. Sargent, since 1890, 


Reversion to Savage Type? 

“Although I have to actual figures 
to verify the statement,” he said "I 
know for a fact that the women vt 
today ane more mannish than the wo- 
men of that time. Women in the 
savage state were so like men in form 
that it was well-nigh impossible to 
tell them apart. ‘I'ten, as civilization 
progressed, their especially feminine 
characteristics became accentuated 
until the exaggeration was almose 
painful to look at. 

“Since that time woman's physique 
has been thoroughly made over. It is 
approximating that of man. The 
sloping shoulders of her grandmoth- 
er’s time have disappeared, They are 
no longer in fashion, They are no 
longer desired, In their place we find 
well-knit, athletic shoulders — broad 
ones. Her back, likewise, is better 


“Wer neck is) thicker and more 
muscular; her limbs more smoothly 
developed; her hands and feet. are 
larger. In every respect of which we 
in making measurements of many wo- 
men daily take note, the woman of 
today is getting to be more like man. 
Her chest has been said to be flatter. 
Il should hardly wish to endorse tint: 
opinion, It is true, however, that Hem 
chest may appear flatter, because, £ 
am glad to say, the corset whieh fore 
merly forced it: upward, has of date 
been somewhat modified. 

“Tt is this modification of the corset: 
which is responsible for the betterment: 
of woman’s figure. 'Mhe tightly laced 
corset is no longer in style but seems: 
to be replaced by the corset. wadst. 
Thus both the upper and lower partesa 
of the trunk have more room, ‘Ihe 
chest is therefore larger and better 
developed. fn certain persons as ine 
Miss De Capetan, who in constantly 
doing trapeze work, and other things 
which tend to develop it, the chest les 
developed to an extreme depree, anc 
is stronger than that of many a mar, 

Athletics Responsible 

“The modification of the corset, aa 
| have said, is ome reason for the 
change in woman's physique, Another 
reason is that within the last twentzy 
years women have done mone ii tho 
way of athletics than they did formes 
ly. ‘Golf, tennis, running and swhir 
ming have done much to improve wou 
man's physical development. — Bielney 
the sports which one naturally takes 
to be masculine, they have the inevi ct 
able result of making those who tet 
dulge in them more like men. 

“For iit is a rule ‘o which one mpaay 
take few exceptions that if a man de 
votes himself to the activities, of 4 
woman, or if a woman devotes heraalt 
to the activities “of a than/ “ete ts 
bound to be a loss of sex, Andit mast 
be admitted that is one of the tendem 
cies of our time, Tt is a result «f 
commercialism, We find a gresat 
many women do work which nen 
ought to do, and vice versa. 

“This approximation of man’s, phy 
siqtie is, from ary anthropologleal 
point of yiew, a bad thing, It is to Wa 
hoped that men and women will mo 
grow to be ‘more alike than they an 


today. ‘he same may be said of mes, 


From the overdeveloped Sandow, mat 
has changed to the effeminate azul 
the foppish being, ‘Ihe assimilatlen 
of tthe sexes by each other isa poo 
sibility to be decried,” 


A TRADITION OF THE TOc) 


STOY FAMILY, 


(‘Manchester Guardian,') 

The traditional story of the rise of 
the Tolstoy family may not he (rae, 
but it is a very good story of its khanf, 
Tt was in the early pant of tthe Scvesi 
teenth century that a certain private 
of the bodyguard was standing sen-atty 
before the entrance to the ‘lsar's fail - 
vale apartments, A nobleman of h ihe 
rank approached and attempted (vo 
force his way past, ‘lhe soldier, tt 
spectfully interposing his Wenfa0t t, 
mentioned the strict. orders he ‘hed 
received, ‘Ihe nobleman hauglaallry 
reminded the man of his name god 
rank, but this had no effeet, ani chs a 
passion the lashed ithe man across. thee 
face with his switel,. At the moc 
ment the door opened and the Hoa 
appeared, and asked the meantigay Col 
the disturbanac, 

The nobleman explained. ras 
T'sar listened carefully tif he fact fea: 
ished, dhen turned to ithe soldier: 

‘You, ‘Tolstoy, were strick by thal 
gentleman for obeying orders, “Inthe 
my cane and strike him as he gsttiect 
you.’ 

‘But, your Majesty,’ interrupted (I le 
nobleman, ‘this man is a commorn soc 
dier,’ 

‘Shen I make him a captain’ 

‘But Loam an officer of your Mt 
jesty’s Vousehold,’ 

‘LT make him a colonel of my> [vile 
Guards,’ 

‘My rank, as your Majesty k-moww, 
is that of general,’ 

‘Then I make him a general tcm, 0 
that the beating you will get will be 
from a man of your own rank’! 

Whether the beating was ox revs 
not returned is not recorded, huest neat 
day the young soldier was comm: 
sioned.a general of the Rossian—arany- 
and created a count of the Emplers,

At the annual meeting of the Ren- 
frew hockey club the other night it 
was announced that $15,000 was paid 
cut in ‘salaries to players last. year. 
“The revenue of course,” the report 
adds, “did not cover expenses but the 
club went into the game for advertis- 
ing purposes, and, not expecting to 
make money, was well satisfied with 
the outcome.” - 

Such policy certainly doesn’t help 
sport. We are plainly told what its 
purpose is. But how does the ad- 
vertisement received help a town? 
Does it bring more: people to live 
there who are worth anything to it? 
The old argument was that by such 
expenditure interest in a game was 
aroused and local players would be 
brought along to keep it going. Such 
an assumption 4s perfectly ground- 


less, as the experience of scores of |- 


Canadian towns and cities that have 
gone into lacrosse and hockey on 
this basis have shown. The Renfrew 
people, however, incidentally do the 
cause of genuine sport a good ser- 
vice by stating their object so plain- 
ly and boastfully. This must have 
the effect of creating general’ dis- 
gust and keeping other places from 
following their example. 


London Referee:—A pentinent an- 
swer to a great many questions is 
that “it all depends upon. circum- 
stances,” and this seems to be the 
answer ¢o the question which is 
now agitating some writers: Wheth- 
er ¢oo much time 1s given up to 
games. Everything depends upon 
how the time would be passed if it 
were otherwise occupied. IJ¢ is far 
better to be idle than to do mischief, 
and the question is hopelessly vague, 


moreover, for one wants to know 


how much constitutes too ~ much? 
A not inconsiderable number of peo- 
ple, for instance, having secured blue 
tickets or red tickets, as the case may 
be, went to see what they regard- 
ed as the attractive show at the Old 
Bailey the other day, where they 
were gratified not only by the spec- 
tacle of Crippen in the dock, but by 
that of a popular musical comedy ac- 
tress somewhere, it appears, in the 
immediate neighborhood of the Lord 
Chief Justice. Visitors to that enter- 
tainment would really have been in- 
finitely better employed watching a 
game of football or attending a race 
meeting. 


ND 


The English racing season came to 
a close two weeks, ago with a small 
meeting at Manchester, and except 
that the closing events might decide 
the supremacy between -Frank Woot- 
ton, the English jockey, and Danny 
Maher, the American, the leaders a- 
mong the winning owners, horses 
and stallions have been apparent for 
seme time. Almost from the start. 
cr at least since the Derby was won 
by Lemberg, Mr. Fairie, owner of 
the great colt, has been on the top 
of the column devoted to winning 
owners, with Lord Derby in second 
place. For a while Lord Rosebery 
held the third position, but. Harry 
Payne Whitney’s string did splendid 
work toward the end, with che re- 
sult that he replaced Lord Rosebery 
in third place, where he finishes with 
a respectable total. It was the dope 
for a long time that che young Amer- 
ican owner would have the credi! 
of winning the most races, for he 
led until very recently, when Locd 
Derby scored some wins which gave 
him the pride of place. 

Out of the 16 leading horses, the 
smallest winner cleared over $15,000. 
Lemberg, winner of the Derby and 
other big races, is easily the best, with 
a total more than twice more than 
kis nearest rival. His total is $119,- 
195, and as he holds all the big four- 
year-old weight for age, and impor- 
tant handicap engagements ‘he should 
prove a valuable cok to Mr. Fairie 
before he quits the track. Swyn- 
ford, who, it will be remembered, 
won the St. Leger, beating Lemberg 
and others, finishes in fourth pace 
and was the chief winner for L-rd 
Derby. He began the season in very 
poor shape, so poor that around Ep- 
som classic he was rated anywhere 
from twenty-five to thirty pounds 
behind Lemberg or Neil Gow. Pe- 
fore gojng into winter quarters he 
won the Liverpool St. Leger, having 


ee 
a a 


THE SATURDAY NEWS. ss 


to be a colt of grand quality: and is 
mcre than liable 20 do. something. 
He is the best-cf the juveniles’ and 
in the stotal for the year he ‘is in 
front of Seaforth, St. Nat, Pietri ana 
Cellini, the quartet) cf youngsters 
who figure. amcng ‘the first sixteen. 
Not one of the five, however, ap- 
proaches the sym made by Bayardo 
when -he-- cleared’ $767,000 as a two- 
year-old. 


A writer in the; Toronto Star has 
seme observances to make which will 
come close ‘home to many of us. 

“Did you ever belong,” he asks, ‘ic 
a junior lacrosse or. baseball team 
in a village when you were a-boy? 
Do you remember the chief enthu- 
siast of the organisation? Perhaps 
he’ was a_ spindle-legged. narrow- 
chested, gentle little lad, but there 
was fire in his eye when the team 
went owt to practice. If the -game 
was basketball he was a failure at 


the bat, but on his weakest hit he- 


ran for. first base and over-it‘as if this 
life depended on getting. to the bag 
before the ball. He put all his boy- 
ish force of will and bodily vitality 
into every play he made. But he did 
more than that. If uniforms were 
wanted he took the matter in hand, 
for he. took. a tremendous pride in 


: JACK WESTERMAN 
In ‘A Royal’ Prisoner,’ to be present ed at the Empire Theatre soon. 


If tthe club’s 
exchequer could not furnish ithe full 
regulation equipment of bats, balls, 
mask, and so on, the enthusiast sold 
his 


skimped himself in pocket money, 


the team’s uniform. 


homing pigeons and_ rabbits, 


and saw that the proper purchases 
were made. Several of the- 
players were rather tough and. care- 
less youngsters. They played like 
lions on the field, but they had n> 
imagination, no permanent enthusi- 
asm. When the team was almost 
ready to drive over to play against 
the juvenile experts of a rival village, 
some of these boys would announce 
that they couldn’t go. They hadn't 
any money, perhaps, or they had 
chores to do at home. Then the en- 
thusiastic would get fiercely busy, 
and round up the team, displaying re- 
sourcefulness worthy of a railroad 
presidem, Perhaps when the bus 
was ready to start, he would discover 
that a careless player had no uniform 
but didn’t think it mattered—he could 
play just as well without it. At this 
the enthusiast would boil over. Play 
a match without a uniform! What 
kind of a team would they have if 
they didn’t even look like a real team! 
Where would be their esprit 
de corps! And he would see that 
the bus didn’t start until that missing 
uniform was on board. Yes, that 
weedy boy was not a very husky in- 
dividual player, but his fierce, burn- 
ing enthusiasm made him the life and 
spirit of his team.” 

Who-ean’t fill out the picture for 
himself? How many there are who 
received their first and best training 
in the handling of men under such 
circumstances as these? I never see 
such a boy-leader bit what I want 
to take hold of him and see to it 
that ke gets every chance to brinz 
the qualities imto play in teal life 


nection with team management. If 
Waterloo was won on the playing- 
vrounds of Eten, how many great 
hrsiness and 


professional. careers 


a 


4 ; per gtr eS ¢ FRig ae ECR Re aan a RTS 
| a walkover for the event,.'He is said’, may. be said“to: start® an“ the baseball 


bes: | 


; of athletics j : 
that are counting for so much in | ctics in the past fifteen years 


diamond ‘cr’ the lacrosse: field? 


The passage of tthe years is brought 
home to us by the statement | that 
1 came across an Eastern ‘paper. the 
other day ‘that Hendrie’s Maritmis 
was néarly fifteen years. old. It was 
in 1898 that the news flashed over 


the wire that the had wom the Futuri- 
ever pulled off the big event. 


He is a beautiful chestnut in. color, 
'and is a model in conformation. He 
is not a big horse in height, as he 
stands but 15.2 hands, but neverthe- 
less he is one of the made-to-order 
kind, and he invariably imparts -his 
excellent qualities to his off-sprins. 
In blood lines, he comes from-a win- 
ning , family on each side of the 
house, as his ‘sire, imp. Candlemas, 
a son of Hermit-Fupsee. (dam of St. 
Blaise), by Marsyas, won many im- 
portant races, including the Epsom 
_-Grand Prize, Royal Stakes, Autumn 
Cup,.ete., and his dam: Biggonet; .a 
daughter: of Bramble: — Bobinet, by 
Brown Dick, was. good enough ta 
win the Withers Stakes at Jerome 
Park. 


Besides the Futurity, Martimas 
won several other important events 
as a 2-year-old, and finished the 'sea- 
son with $£41,140°to his credit. 


As_a 3-year-old he won the Cana- 
dian Derby and ‘Nautilus. Stake, and 
the next year he won the Toronto 
Cup and Spencer Handicap. 

In itis career on the--turf, which 

extended over a period of three years 
he won $52,009, which is the largest 
amount ever won by any Canadian: 
; owned horse. 
Martimas has proved himself a suc- 
beens sire, having to his credit such 
good performers as Kelvin (King’s 
Plate winner), Shimonese (King’s 
i Plate winner), Glimmer, Kelpie, etc., 
j all noted winners. As evidence ot 
his popularity as a sire, it might be 
Stated that a yearling filly hy Maréj- 
mas and out of Lyddite, therefore 
an own Sister to Shimonese, recently 
sold for $1,050 at auction in Toronto, 
and it is pleasing to note that de 
buyer is a Canadian, who shows in 
confidence in the future of the tho: 
roughbred in this country. 

Martimas has been kept a sa pti- 
vate stallion at Valley Farm, Hamil- 
ton, otherwise he certainly wontld 
have had many more winners to his 
credit, as his Opportunity in the stud 
has naturally been limited. 

The recent article in this depart- 
mem on the bearing which an ath- 
lete’s system of training has on his 
ability to ‘come back” has aroused 
much interest and this clipping from 
a contribution to Outing by Dr. Hut- 
chinson has been sent me. 

“There is, therefore,” . he writes, 
‘absolutely no inherent or mneces- 
sary danger to the heart in athletics. 
It is only unintelligent ,0r mistaken 
systems of training that are at fault. 
43 certain methods of training have 
been, it may be broadly stated, even 
admitting the charges brought 
against athletics of deaths and 
life-long disabilities from over- 
etrained and dilated hearts, that the 
extraordinary growth and popularity 


have benefitted, at feast ten times 
as they have injured. The worst 
and most dangerous from of exercise 
for the heart is none at all, 


HELPLESS CHPPLE 


“FROM, RHEUMATISM. 


Five Boxes Of“Fruit-a-tives” Cured Her 


. 4 HoME PLace, ToRONTO, DEc. 15th, 1909. 


ac le sufferer from Rheumatism for nearly a year, and my right 
arm oe ar and the pain was fearful. All down the right side, the pain 
was dreadful, and I could hardly move for the agony. I was treated: by. two: - 
physicians but their medicine did me no good, and I.tried numerous other: 
remedies but received no benefit. I -was-simply a helpless cripple and suffered 
from Rheumatism all during last winter. 

I saw ‘‘Fruit-a-tives” advertised in ‘‘The Telegram’’and. decided to try this: 
remedy. \ After I had taken one box, I was much better and the pain less, and I 
continued the treatment with good hopes. When I had taken three boxes, I was. . 
so well that I could use my arm again and the pain was practically gone. After 
I had taken five boxes, I was entirely well again—no pain—no suffering—and' 

as I ever was. + : aot 
ah Sheed a case by '‘Fruit-a-tives"”. was indeed splendid-because all the. & 
doctors failed to even relieve me of my sufferings. 

For the sake of others who may suffer from this terrible disease, Rheuma- 
tism, I give you permission to publish this statement.”’ 

Mrs. LIZZIE BAXTER. 


‘Frnit-a-tives” is the only remedy, that actually cures Rheumatism, and 
Sciatica because 'Fruit-a-tives'™ is the ony medicine that actually. prevents Uric 
Acid being formed in any quantity in the body. If. there is no excess of uric 
acid in the blood, there can be no Rheumatism. ; uy ANE ; 

“*Prnit-a-tives” keeps the stomach clean—the liver active—the bowels re fr 
—the Thiney strong and the skin healthy. These are the organs that rid the body 
of all waste. When ‘‘Fruit-a-tives’’ 90 regulates the system that all waste is 
eliminated, then there can be no waste or ‘‘urea’”’ to be changed into uric acid. 
Thus, there can be no uric acid in the blood, to inflame nerves and cause the 
which we know by the names of Rheumatism, ‘Sciatica, Lumbago, Neu: 
“Frnit-a-tives’”? cure every trace of Rheumatism, Pain In The Back, Swollem 
Hands and Feet, and other troubles due to the blood being poisoned by uric acid; 

If you are subject to Rheumatism, cure yourself now with ‘‘Fruit-a-tives’” 
and be free of pain this winter. ee

1 Love's light glows the longest 


When’ a newspaper receives letters 
in which tthe correspondent makes 
an appeal on the strength of being 
“one of your original- subscribers,” 
the editor lifts this chin at least an 
inch and a half and makes-the mental 
observation that he is some journa- 
list at last. Such a letter is not in- 
frequently laid on the Louger’s desk 
nowadays. One this week made a re- 
quest for -republication -of Judge 
Mulligan’s famous poem on Ken- 
tucky. The hunt for it led away 
back to one ‘of first issues of The 
Saturday News but here it is: 


The moonlight: falls the softest 
In Kentucky. 

The summer days come oftest 
In Kentucky; 

Friendship is the strongest 


Yet wrong is always wrongest 
In Kentucky. 


Life's burdens bear the lightest. 
In Kentucky, - 

The home fires burn the brightest 
In Kentucky, 

While players are the keenest 

Cards come out: the meanest 

The pocket empities cleanest 
In Kentucky. , ; 


The sun shines ever bright est 
In Kentucky, 

The breezes whisper lightest 
“In ‘Kentucky: st 

Plain girls are ‘the fewest, 

Maidens’ eyes are the bluest, 

Their: little hearts are truest 
‘In Kentucky. 


Ayers 


Orators are the grandest 
In Kentucky. ; 
Officials are blandes: 
In Kentucky. 
Boys are all the fliest, 
Dangers ever nighest, 
And taxes are the hizhest 
In Kentucky. 


The blue grass waves the bluest 
Yet bluebloods arc the fewest 
In Kentucky. 
Moonshine is the cleanest, - 
By no. means the dearest, 
And yet it acts the queerest 
In Kentucky. 


The dove’ notes are the saddest 
In Kentucky. 

The streams dance on the gladdest 
In Kentucky. 

Hip pockets- are the thickest, 

Pistol hands the slickest, 

The cylinder turns the quickest 
In Kentucky. 


The songbirds are the sweetest 
In Kentucky. 
The tthoroughbreds are the fleetest 
In Kentucky. 
Mountains tower proudest, 
Thunder peals the loudest, 
The landscape is the grandest, 
And politics the d t 
In Kentucky. 


Of course Judge Mulligan wrote 
some years before Alberta came on 
the political scene. 


No one needs to be ttold what 
makes Kentucky famous and it i3 
not remarkable, therefore, that it 
makes an appeal to the imagination 
of a latge section of the people of 
this continent. Its ;famous- toast 
proclaims its titles to distinction. 
Here’s to tthe State of Kentucky, 

The State where I was born, 
Wher tthe corn is full of kernels 

And the colonels full of corn. 

But sometimes it gets beyond the 
colonel stage and produces a first- 
class general. Such a one is Simon B. 
Buckner, a veteran of the Mexican 
and civil wars and once candidate 
for vice-president. At the age of 
87 he describes himself as the hap- 
piest man in the world. 

The other day he tndertook to 
tell-in a public address what under- 
lay this happiness, and here is wnat 
he said:— 


THE SATURDAY NEWS 


“TUT 


HAVE YOU TRIED 


National 


PAGE ELEVEN 


vation to-day as any one could wish 
{ raise my own tobacco and I have 
a fine mint bed, and my old dog Gen 
eral wags his tail every time I walk 
into the front yard. There is a good. 
spring just outside the door. The 
water just rushes from ithe rocks, 
and is as cool and pure as any water 
in the world. Along the banks of 
the little stream that. trickles from, 
the spring grows the finest’ mint id? 
the world. This water and tthis mint 
when combined with a little of Ken-! 
tuck’s best spirits make the fienest 


‘| boy?” . 


mint julep in the world.” 

Where in acient or modern litera- 
ture is a more idyllic picture to ve 
found than this? : 


ee 


Most wonderful things-are happen- 
pening in’ Canada these days. The 
following paragraph is taken from 
the London. Yachtsman of :recent 
date: 

“The steam yacht Sheelah, owned 
by Mr. J.. Ross, of Montreal, and 
commanded by Captain Herbert, 2f 
Cowes, has arrived at Southampton 
after an eventful voyage. She took 
her owner aboard at Quebec, and 
went up to the Great Lakes as far 
as Port Arthur, on Lake Superior, 
passing through forty-eight locks, 
and crossing over Wiagara. The} 
Sheelah is the longest vessel that 
has ever passed over. the famous 
falls. On the journey down — the 
yacht shot the rapids, a hazardous 
trip, which; thanks to the skill with 
which she was navigated, was suc- 
cessfully accomplished.” 

What will these Montneal. million- 
aires do next? Pe 


As the train neared Strathcona, 
the colored porter approached the 
jovial-faced gentleman, saying, with 
a smile:_ 

“Shall Ah brush yo’ off; sah?” 

“No,” he replied; “I prefer to get 
off in the usual: manner.” - 


“Why are you’ weeping, little 
“IT broke de pitcher.” 
“Well, there’s no use crying over 
spilt milk.” a 
“G-wan! Dis wuz. beer!” 


A fashionable. photographer — has 
undoubtedly attained the pinnacle of 
tactful achievement. A woman with 
a decided squint came to him for a 
photograph. : 

“Will you permit me,” he said 
promptly, “to take your portrait in 
profile? There is a certain shyness 
about one of your eyes which is’ as 
difficult in ant as it is fascinating in 
nature.” o 


—<$—$<= 


“Your sister’s a long time about 
making her appearance,” suggested 
the caller. “Well,” ‘said the little 
brother, 'she’d be a sight if she came 
down without making it.” 


- 


“Oh, that my son should wish to 
marry an actress!” shrieked the 
proud patrician mother. “Now, ma; 
don’t ttake on so,” beseeched the un- 
dutiful heir. “She isn’t really-an ac- 
tress, she only thinks she is,” 


———- 


She (during a squabble)—Didin't 
you promise before we were married 
to try hard to be worthy of me?” 
He—"Yes,, and what's the result— 
I overdid the job and tade myself 
a hang sight better than you de- 
serve.” 


“T see that you have a young man 
stenographer.” 

“Yes,” 

“Don’t you think a pretty girl ste- 
nographer adds a great deal to the 
attractiveness of an office?” 

“I suppose she does, but I can’t 
dictate tto a woman, somehow. I 
spose it’s because I have been mar- 
tied so long.” 

“Queer, isntt it, that a lawyer 
should be such a lover of dogs?” 

“What is there queer about it?” 

“It is more natural-like for a law- 
yer. to become attached to something 
more in the fee-line species.” 


Don't be a Johnny. 

A fellow has to hustle mow 
and do his daily chores, 

And furthermore be careful how 
He hangs around stage doors. 

The stage door is a garish sput, 
A risky place to lurk, 

An loafing there has put a lot 
Of fellows out of work. 


“I am living in the same old log|The same is true from Maine to Cal. 


cabin on my farm in old Hart county 
that I was born in. That cabin is 
103 years old. My father built it, 
and it is in as good a state of preser- 


And we have proofs to bring. 

Just take a glance at Portugal— 

It happened to a king. , 
—Louisville Courier-Journal.

TWHK INSPECTORS 


G. T. P. 


(Revised Version) 
An ant met a hopper on the road— 
_AIl frosty was the way; 
“Pass on,” said the ant; “do not un- 
load 
Your ttale of woe today; 
Don't tell me how you've tried in 
vain 
To save a winter’s store; 
Your hard luck yarn gives me a pain— 
It goes with me no more. 


“You've rioted all season long— 
You scamp, deny it not— 
I’ve theard your music rising strong 
When summer days were hot; 
You've failed to garner when you 
might, 
And saved not when you ought; 
Pass on, and die tthis very night— 
For wastrels I have naught.” 


WINDSOR HOTEL 


Under new management 


“Good friend, you err,” the hopper 
said; 
“Fall finds me not distressed; 
I’ve food in plenty, tabloided, 
In pockets of my vest; 
At silken garments that I wear 
Let envy fill your lamps; 
Ive wealth, and ne’er a carking care— 
I picked the baseball champs!” 
—Arthur Chapman in Denver Repub- 
lican, 


HE KNOWS WHAT FIXED 
HIM UP 


Dodd’s Kidney Pills Cured §. D: 
Vickar’s Lumbago.

Great Canadian Kidney Remedy 
Made Short Work of His Trouble. 
Edenbridge, Sask. Dec. 12. (Spe- 

cial.)—"It was one box of Dodd's 

Kidney Pills that fixéd me tip.” This 

is tthe cheerful answer Mr Satn. D. | 


Commodious and luxurious accomodation 


J. A. BEAUCHAMP

Proprietor 


King Edward Hotel 


Vickar ig giving his inquiring neigth- 
bors in the 
around her ktows 
years he has been 
Lumbago. Now he’s 
well again. 

“My Lumbago developed from 4a 
cold,” Mr. Vickar goes on to Say. 
“My head would ache. I was always 
tired and nervous. I liad a_ bittce 
taste in my mouth in the morning, 
was troubled with dizzy spells and 
was always ‘thirsty. The doctor told 
me 1 had Lumbago, but did nat help 
me vety miich. Dodd’s Kidney Pills 
cured me,” 

Dodd’s Kidney Pills went straight 
to the root of the trouble. ‘They cur- 
ed his kidneys. The cured Kidneys 
strained the uric acid out fof 'the 
blood, and Mr. Vickar’s Lumbago 
vanished. Dodd’s Kidney Fills are 
no cure-all. They simply cure sick 
kidneys. They never fail to do that.
